These are F/K/T awards that were previously auto-classified as "training" based on activity code.
Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

title: Deciphering the Role of Gut Microbiome in Inflammatory Bowel Disease Using a Canine Patient-Specific Gut-on-a-Chip
org_name: WASHINGTON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The role of the gut microbiome in both canine and human inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) has been poorly understood because a patient specific experimental model that can reflect genetic heterogeneity and host- microbiome dynamics do not exist. My research proposes to develop a translational, patient-specific IBD Chip by integrating a microfluidic gut-on-a-chip and canine patient-derived intestinal organoids to recapitulate epithelium-microbiome-immune axis. The ultimate goal is to map the intestinal epithelium-microbiome crosstalk relevant to IBD by leveraging the single-cell multi-omics analysis while validating the findings with RNA in situ hybridization, which may lead to a broader impact on uncovering the underlying disease mechanism and developing new targeted anti-...

activity_code: K23
title: Developing a daily life measure of depression for persons with aphasia and examining its relation with communicative confidence
org_name: REHABILITATION INSTITUTE OF CHICAGO D/B/A SHIRLEY RYAN ABILITYLAB
phr: The proposed study focuses on developing and validating a daily life measure of depression for persons with aphasia and investigating its association with communicative confidence. application_id: 10738754
activity_code: K01
title: Novel animal models to dissect how lung macrophages contribute to SARS-CoV2 alveolar pathology and respiratory failure
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative SARS-CoV2 is an ongoing global pandemic that targets the most vulnerable members of our society with persistent, overwhelming inflammation of the lungs that can eventually cause respiratory failure and death. However, the specific factors that act locally in the lungs to determine disease severity remain unknown. These studies will contribute powerful new tools to obtain clinically relevant information about macrophages, the predominant immune cells in SARS-CoV2 infection, including whether they prevent repair of lung damage during infection, and whether they may be potential drug targets to reduce disease severity and death in humans infected with SARS-CoV2.

application_id: 10745340
activity_code: K01
title: Acamprosate pharmacogenomics: iPSC based model of alcohol use disorder
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Acamprosate is an FDA approved drug for the treatment of alcohol use disorder (AUD) in the United States but only a small proportion of AUD patients respond to this drug. The proposed studies have been designed to apply a systematic genome-wide approach using patient-derived stem cells as a novel cellular model system to identify molecular and genomic signatures for AUD as well as mechanisms underlying individual variation in response to acamprosate. The ultimate goal is to advance our understanding of disease pathophysiology and drug action responsible for variation in acamprosate response in AUD patients.

activity_code: K01
title: Improving the Quality of Prenatal Care for Low-Income, Urban, Black Women
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Black women are three times more likely than White women to die from early-onset preeclampsia (EOP; <34 weeks' gestation; a disorder of pregnancy characterized by hypertension and proteinuria) and other childbirth- related issues. Yet we do not know why the disparities exist and how evidence-based practices are implemented to reduce disparities in preeclampsia. The proposed study will identify processes of care to prevent EOP, provider's barriers and facilitators to implementing evidence-based treatment to prevent EOP, and women's awareness of preeclampsia and evidence-based treatment to prevent EOP.

application_id: 10745973
activity_code: T32
title: Translational research training in cardiovascular science
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Surgeon-scientists in training have competing clinical responsibilities but must compete for contentious funding lines, which means that state-of-the art training is critical for producing the best surgeon-scientists. With the aging of the population, the number of patients with cardiovascular disease is markedly increasing. The proposed program, with its overarching focus on vascular biology and three distinct tracks including (1) basic/translational, (2) health sciences and (3) biomedical engineering, under multi-disciplinary preceptor guidance, will produce the next generation of surgeon-scientist leaders who will pave the way to new discoveries in human health.

application_id: 10746007
activity_code: K08
title: Amygdala modulation of volitional versus automatic breathing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) is the most common cause of death in patients with chronic refractory epilepsy, and there are no known preventive strategies. Recent studies indicate the inciting event in SUDEP is seizure-induced loss of breathing, which occurs with seizure spread to the amygdala. This proposal will identify neural mechanisms by which the amygdala influences the brain’s respiratory control network during normal breathing, which is critical to designing safe and effective new therapies for sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) and other neurological disorders that affect breathing.

activity_code: K01
title: Behavioral and neural properties of spatial direction comprehension in aging and amestic mild cognitive impairment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Spatial navigation, a fundamental human behavior which facilitates a safe and independent life, is among the first behaviors to decline in healthy aging and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias, greatly contributing to poor health outcomes and a loss of independence in these populations. title: Elucidating genetic mechanisms of Clostridioides difficile pathogenesis and patient immune manipulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative The interplay between a patient’s immune response and the genetic background of their infecting strain can lead to severe Clostridioides difficile infection. title: Understanding spillover potential of European and African bat sarbecoviruses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sarbecoviruses have crossed from bats to humans twice in the last two decades via viruses that emerged from distinct locations on the sarbecovirus evolutionary tree. activity_code: F31
title: Cellular and molecular mechanisms of cold-adapted mechanosensation in the hibernating squirrel
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While cold exposure blocks peripheral sensory signaling in most mammals, the hibernating thirteen-lined ground squirrel preserves touch sensation (mechanosensation) at a body temperature of 2-4°C. This proposal utilizes physiological and molecular methods to identify the modifications at dorsal root ganglion ion channels underlying cold-resistant mechanosensation. Understanding cellular mechanisms of extreme thermal tolerance in the mammalian nervous system will provide new targets for the mitigation of neuronal damage following organ supercooling or perioperative hypothermia.

title: Orbitofrontal modulation of dopamine during value-based decision-making
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Our previous experiences can influence our future choices in many ways, for example by biasing choices towards actions that have been successful in the past (“retrospective decision-making”), or by choosing actions that you think will be successful based on careful mental planning (“inferential decision-making”). Biologically, retrospective decision-making is thought to be mediated by dopamine, though recent work has also suggested a role for dopamine in inferential decision-making. This proposal seeks to describe novel neural circuits that underlie inferential decision-making, the results of which will have applications in neuroscience, psychology, and machine learning.

application_id: 10763778
activity_code: K23
title: The Study of Families with Heritable Crohn's Disease to Support Rational Design of Microbiodata-Based Therapies
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Because the etiology of Crohn's disease (CD) is not well understood, many of its current treatments are focused on systemic immunosuppression, which can increase the risk of serious infections and malignancies1,2. The proposed study will evaluate the relative contributions of genetic risk and of the gut microbiome to CD development, with a focus on identifying microbial signatures of individuals who have never developed CD despite having high genetic risk. We will study whether these microbial signatures and their associated protective phenotype are transferable, with the long-term goal of impacting the development of safer, more effective microbiota-based therapeutics to treat or prevent CD.

title: Discovery of Novel Genetic Variants Underlying Unexplained Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy by Whole Genome Sequencing, RNA-seq and iPSC modeling
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is the most common inherited cardiovascular disease with substantial morbidity and mortality, and the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young people, for which, yet no effective treatment is available, and the etiologies for 40-70% of cases are not known. My studies suggested a role for non-coding variants in pathogenesis of unexplained HCM. title: Investigating mechanisms activating the selective autophagy of lysosomes

application_id: 10766741
activity_code: K01
title: Particulate Matter (PM) Metals and Racial Disparity in Cardiovascular (CVD) Risk Factors
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Exposure to Particulate Matter (PM) metals during pregnancy is known to be harmful to both mother and the new born. Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is one such harmful effect of exposure to PM metals. In the natural environment we are always exposed to mixtures of chemicals with different toxicities. phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Every year, millions of people in the U.S. need some sort of endodontic treatment due to dental pulp infection and necrosis. title: Cortical mechanisms and modulation of somatosensation in older adults with foot sole somatosensory impairments
org_name: HEBREW REHABILITATION CENTER FOR AGED
phr: Project Narrative In older adults, diminished sensation of the legs and feet is highly prevalent and causes poor balance and reduced mobility. activity_code: K01
title: Characterizing Alzheimer's Risk in Retired Night Shift Workers: Cognitive Function, Brain Volume, and Brain Bioenergetics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and related dementias (ADRD) are the leading U.S. health concern of the 21st century, and night shift work is an increasingly recognized risk factor for AD/ADRD. The proposed study is the first in a broader program of research that will characterize AD/ADRD-related cognitive function, brain structure, and brain bioenergetics in retired night shift workers compared to retired day workers, generating evidence on the potential “scarring” of shift work exposure on Alzheimer’s disease risk. Findings will inform future experimental approaches to identify behavioral and pharmacological interventions to prevent and attenuate the clinical course of AD/ADRD in a large portion of U.S workers.

application_id: 10769856
activity_code: K08
title: Myeloid TLR4 epigenetic regulation and signaling in accelerating venous thrombus resolution
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE When blood clots form in the large veins, a common condition known as deep vein thrombosis (DVT), patients are treated with blood thinning medications, which carry the risk of bleeding and are ineffective at dissolving the clot, ultimately leading to the risk of recurrent DVT and chronic leg swelling, pain and ulcers (post thrombotic syndrome). Immune cells (monocytes/macrophages) help to break down the thrombus, and accelerate this process without bleeding risk. This research proposal is intended to evaluate the role of a common cellular signaling pathway, TLR4, in monocytes/macrophages in directing thrombus resolution, assess potential as a therapeutic strategy, and provide advanced immunologic training for a promising young surgeon-scientist.

title: Cortical Computations Underlying Planning, Generating, and Orchestrating Complex Cognitive-Motor Sequences
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Activities required for daily living usually involve planning and executing long sequences of movements, which are orchestrated through interactions between a large group or cortical and sub-cortical brain structures. Unfortunately, the ability to perform such movement sequences is significantly compromised as a result of neurological disease and/or injury (e.g., Parkinson’s, dystonia, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, etc.). This project aims to understand the neural computations and mechanisms of three key brain regions, including their interactions, which drive movements over long timescales.

title: Immune System Aging in Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease: Epigenetics, biologic aging, and heightened immune states in a population-based study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Key aging-related immune system changes (immunosenescence) combined with chronic, low-level inflammation states, termed inflamm-aging, have been increasingly related to neurodegenerative changes, both in the central nervous system and peripherally. The aim of this proposal is to combine advanced statistical methods and a systems biology modeling approach with high-throughput omics markers (blood-based epigenetics and metabolomics) to study immune system aging in neurodegenerative disease (AD and PD) patients relative to community-based controls. And importantly, relate markers of heightened immune states and faster inflamm- aging to longitudinal disease progression, including symptom development and multiple metabolomics measurements over time.

application_id: 10774644
activity_code: K23
title: Development of a Novel Cannabis Brief Intervention for Frequently-Using Emerging Adults
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Emerging adults (age 18-25) who frequently use cannabis are at an increased risk for cannabis-related problems and developing cannabis use disorder (CUD). Brief interventions (BIs) have been the gold-standard for heavy and problematic alcohol use in emerging adults; however, evidence is mixed, and the quality of studies is low for similar brief interventions targeting frequent cannabis use, suggesting additional rigor is needed to develop and test potentially efficacious cannabis brief interventions. The goal of the proposed study is to develop and evaluate the feasibility, acceptability, and preliminary efficacy of a novel cannabis brief intervention targeting theory- and empirically-based mechanisms associated with frequent cannabis use in emerging adults.

title: Role of selective autophagy in aging and neurodegeneration: a small molecule approach
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autophagy is a recycling process with the ability to recruit selective type of cytosolic components like lipid droplets and protein aggregates for degradation. While accumulating evidence indicates that these selective types of autophagy play crucial roles in cellular health maintenance with application to age-related diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, the regulatory functions of selective autophagy remain elusive. title: Network-Targeted Neuromodulation for Nicotine Dependence in Schizophrenia
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Tobacco use is the top preventable cause of early mortality in schizophrenia, and smoking cessation treatments are significantly less effective in schizophrenia than in the general population. I determined that in schizophrenia, nicotine use is linked to disorganization in a brain network (the Default Mode Network) and used pharmacology and noninvasive neuromodulation to show that manipulating this network temporarily changes craving for nicotine. This project will determine if this network can be changed in an enduring way that leads to decreased nicotine use in schizophrenia.

org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic skin wounds in patients with type II diabetes do not heal, as they are stalled at an inflammation phase without any growth of blood vessels that would typically close the gap. title: Dissecting the contribution of glutamatergic ventral pallidal neurons to the aversive state of opioid withdrawal
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE With repeated opioid use, a driving factor for drug taking becomes the desire to avoid the aversive state of withdrawal, instead of the acute rewarding effects of the drug. However, it is not clear how chronic opioid use alters neural circuits involved in aversion processing to initiate and sustain this aversive withdrawal state. The goal of this project is to understand how chronic opioid use induces plasticity in brain circuits involved in aversion processing, and how these changes drive the aversive state of opioid withdrawal; outcomes of this project will ultimately inform targeted therapies in the treatment of drug withdrawal.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES

title: Optimization and mechanisms of electrical neurostimulation in mouse models of epilepsy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Drug-resistant epilepsy is a devastating neurological disorder that afflicts approximately 1 million people in the United States who have limited effective treatment options. Brain stimulation is a novel therapy for epilepsy that is adjustable and generally well-tolerated; however, the optimal protocols and underlying mechanisms of neurostimulation in epilepsy remain unknown. title: Decreasing Polypharmacy in Older Adults with Curable Cancers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Polypharmacy, or taking multiple concurrent medications, is very common in older adults with cancer undergoing curative-intent chemotherapy. Polypharmacy is associated with adverse outcomes including receiving less chemotherapy, which in turn can affect cancer-related survival. “Deprescribing,” or the planned discontinuation of medications, is an intervention that can address polypharmacy, but it has not been studied in older adults with cancer receiving curative-chemotherapy; this proposal refines and tests two deprescribing intervention strategies in these patients.

phr: Project Narrative The Summer Undergraduate Research Experience in Aging and Neurologic Diseases at Washington University recruits undergraduate students with diverse backgrounds and disciplinary interests for an eight-week introduction to research on the prevention and treatment of neurologic disorders in older adults. Students join a laboratory focused on one of three neurological conditions (stroke, Parkinson’s disease, or dementia) for a closely mentored research experience. They also gather together for lectures, discussions, field trips, and professional development seminars to nurture their knowledge, skills, professional identity, and momentum as scholars.

activity_code: F31
title: Extracellular Matrix Remodeling and the Control of Invasive Lobular Carcinoma Progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) is the second most common subtype of breast cancer and demonstrates unique features supporting its highly invasive and metastatic nature. Despite this, lack of effective models has left ILC largely understudied with no insight into the mechanics underlying its invasive potential. This proposal aims to dissect the tumor-extracellular matrix interactions of invading ILC cells, in search of novel therapeutic targets.

application_id: 10811663
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Fos and the BAF Complex in Neuronal Activity-Dependent Chromatin Remodeling and Gene Expression
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Variants in genes encoding multiple subunits of the BAF chromatin remodeling complex are a cause of genetic neurodevelopmental disorders. The BAF complex has been shown to interact with Fos, a major activity- dependent transcription factor known to be important in neurodevelopmental processes, but how Fos and the BAF complex regulate neuronal activity-dependent genetic programs remains unclear. This project will study the role of Fos and the BAF complex in regulating activity-dependent processes and how these are perturbed in a model of BAF-related neurodevelopmental disease.

application_id: 10812371
activity_code: K23
title: Approach Bias Modification for Co-Occurring Cannabis and Alcohol Use among Treatment-Seeking Adolescents
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescence is a period of considerable development, making the adolescent brain particularly vulnerable to negative effects of cannabis and alcohol use. The proposed research will test an innovative intervention among adolescents seeking-treatment for substance use that aims to modify automatic approach biases for cannabis and alcohol, which are hypothesized precipitating and maintaining factors for adolescent substance use. The modification of implicit cognitive biases via training may be a useful adjunct to existing interventions and could reduce the consequences associated with adolescent substance use.

application_id: 10813707
activity_code: F30
title: The Oncogenic functions of YAP/TAZ in Renal Cell Carcinoma
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Renal cell carcinoma (RCC), the 8th most common form of adult cancer in the US, is in need of novel therapeutic strategies to overcome resistance to existing therapies. My preliminary studies have revealed that deregulation of the Hippo-YAP/TAZ pathway represents a major molecular hallmark in RCC, and is a key mechanism used by RCC cells to maintain their transcriptional homeostasis. Employing patient-derived RCC tumor models and clinically viable inhibitors, my study will establish the therapeutic potential and mechanisms of actions for YAP/TAZ-based therapies in RCC.

application_id: 10813718
activity_code: F32
title: Impact of paternal care on stress-coping behaviors and neuropeptide systems
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Neglect, abandonment, abuse, and other forms of early life adversity significantly increase risk for psychiatric disorders, such as depression, by shifting developmental brain trajectories and sensitizing individuals to subsequent stress. This proposal tests the hypothesis that paternal deprivation alters the lateral septum to respond to social stress experienced later in life via neural, epigenetic, and transcriptomic mechanisms that act on the function of the oxytocin and vasopressin systems. This work will contribute to a novel understanding of stress susceptibility and can provide insight to better inform new therapeutic strategies to understand the developmental differences in how sociality is altered by early life adversity.

application_id: 10813751
activity_code: K08
title: Gamma delta T-cell immunotherapy for T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Current treatment regimens are not adequate for patients with relapsed T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T- ALL). We propose a novel approach to target T-ALL by using innate-like cytotoxic gamma delta (γδ) T cells and will test two strategies, one utilizing cellular stress modulation as a means to sensitize T-ALL to γδ T cell- mediated killing, and the other through the use of a CD5-directed chimeric antigen receptor (CAR). Research outlined in this proposal will provide invaluable preclinical data necessary to develop γδ T cell-based strategies to treat T-ALL.

title: The Women-Centered Opioid Recovery Project: Advancing women's engagement in care for opioid use disorder through development of a novel recovery navigation system led by women in recovery
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The opioid crisis has escalated dramatically among women, but women face many unique challenges in obtaining treatment for opioid use disorder (OUD), highlighting the importance of women-centered strategies for engagement in care. To address these gendered needs and barriers to care, we propose a novel women- centered recovery navigation system. The proposed research will 1) identify key barriers to engagement in care for women with OUD, as well as potential solutions; 2) develop and evaluate a pilot women-centered recovery navigator program; and 3) develop and evaluate a web-based recovery platform to engage women with OUD in care.

activity_code: F31
title: Role of the central nucleus of the amygdala during ethanol-rewarded instrumental tasks
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The desire to consume alcohol is driven by motivation; it is commonly believed that this motivation is altered in those with substance use disorders. The central nucleus of the amygdala is one potential neural substrate for modulating action selection, both seeking and drinking, based on changes in motivation. The proposed research seeks to explore the neural activity pattern in the central nucleus of the amygdala during instrumental tasks rewarded with ethanol to gain a clearer picture of this region’s role in motivated behaviors.

title: Building Early Awareness and Research in Science (BEARS)
org_name: CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY SAN MARCOS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE BEARS is founded upon the knowledge that a diverse biomedical workforce is necessary to address the complex problems in biomedical research. Underrepresented minorities contribute to solving biomedical problems by increasing innovation, cooperation and productivity in research settings. As there is a strong need to reduce the racial and socioeconomical disparities in health, underrepresented minorities bring cultural competence and understand the issues among their demographic groups, which helps build trust with disadvantaged communities and improves the outreach of science among the general public.

application_id: 10817728
activity_code: K01
title: Health Impacts of Prehospital Pain Management for Injured Older Adults
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Injury is a leading cause of death and disability of older adults in the United States and older adults account for one-third of 911 calls for injury, but little is known about the health impact of emergency medical services (EMS) care for this population. This application will support a new investigator who aspires to a research career focused EMS and surgical care of injured older adults, especially the impact of prehospital EMS care on long-term health and functional status following injury diagnosis. The award will enable the investigator to develop knowledge and skills necessary to conduct independent research investigating patterns of care and health outcomes for injured older adults, which will lead to more effective treatment of this population.

title: Developing the microbiome-metabolome as a targeted therapy for obesity-associated asthma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Obesity-associated asthma affects approximately 8 million adults in the United States. Obese adults with late- onset, nonatopic asthma experience more severe symptoms and fewer days of asthma control, resulting in increased asthma-related hospitalizations and reduced quality of life. This proposal will improve treatment options for obesity-associated asthma by developing the microbiome-metabolome and its involvement in inflammatory signaling along the gut-lung axis as a novel targeted therapy.

activity_code: F31
title: Mechanistic and Therapeutic Role of the TLR4 Signaling Pathway in T1D
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: Project narrative Our lab has published that an agonistic TLR4/MD2 antibody (TLR4-Ab) successfully reverses acute T1D in diabetic mice, activates myeloid derived suppressor cells, and has prolonged endosomal sequestration. However, the mechanism by which TLR4-Ab can reverse T1D is still unclear. Our proposed study will determine the role of TLR4-Ab structure, which downstream TLR4 signaling is important for MDSC differentiation, and begin the translation of this therapy from mice to humans by studying novel human anti- TLR antibodies.

application_id: 10820136
activity_code: F30
title: Determining Sox10-mediated plasticity in irradiated salivary gland cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Over 500,000 head and neck cancer patients receive therapeutic radiation each year, resulting in irreversible damage to the salivary glands, salivary hypofunction, and subsequent xerostomia (dry mouth syndrome). As current strategies to mitigate xerostomia fail to address the underlying mechanism of dysfunction, new therapies that long-term repair the injured gland are necessary to address this unmet clinical need. Given that Sox10 is involved in SG epithelial stem/progenitor cell self-renewal and differentiation processes, here we aim to determine whether Sox10 can induce plasticity in irradiated salivary gland ductal cells.

title: Training Medical Students in NIDDK Research

application_id: 10820766
activity_code: F32
title: The role of retrotransposonable elements in neurodevelopment and disease
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Retrotransposonable elements (RTEs) which are genomic elements that have the ability to replicate and insert themselves into new genomic locations through RNA intermediates, are largely silenced in most cell types, but are active during neurodevelopment. Overly active RTEs have been correlated with a number of neurodevelopmental disorders, but discrete mechanistic roles remain elusive. This proposal aims to understand whether RTE activity regulates neurodevelopment, the mechanisms by which overactive RTEs can cause diseases, and whether inhibiting RTE activity can prevent neurodevelopmental disorders such as microcephaly.

activity_code: K01
title: Longitudinal associations between neighborhood greenspace and brain aging in cognitively normal older adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research will investigate if exposure to greater neighborhood greenspace in early, mid, and late- life is associated with healthy brain aging as measured via regional brain volumes and longitudinal decline on multiple cognitive domains. The study will assess whether there are critical periods in which greenspace exposure may be most beneficial, as well as potential effect modification by sex, race, apolipoprotein E (genetic risk factor for Alzheimer’s disease), and geographic region. The ultimate goal is to provide evidence for community-level interventions that can increase cognitive resilience and healthy behaviors throughout the lifespan, promoting healthy brain aging and allowing older adults to age in place.

activity_code: K01
title: Sleep and Circadian Rhythms in Adolescence: Epigenetic Pathways of Cardiometabolic Risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescence is a vulnerable time period when insufficient and mistimed sleep may affect cardiometabolic risk factors including adiposity, high blood pressure, and insulin resistance. To understand which aspects of sleep to intervene on, there is an urgent need to disentangle the independent contributions of short sleep duration and delayed sleep timing (proxy for circadian rhythm misalignment) to cardiometabolic risk. Examining the epigenetic pathways that link sleep duration and timing with prospective cardiometabolic risk provides a novel way to distinguish these relationships and to gain mechanistic insights.

title: The role of retinoic acid signaling in patterning the human cerebral cortex
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Different areas of the brain are composed of unique cell types and connectivity patterns that subserve their unique functional roles, and disruption of areal patterning during development can lead to neurologic and neuropsychiatric disorders such as autism and schizophrenia. Recent studies have implicated a role for retinoic acid, derived from Vitamin A, in patterning of the cerebral cortex, and this proposal will explore the mechanism by which this areal specification occurs. activity_code: K01
title: Predicting risk of cardiotoxicity among young and emerging adult breast cancer patients from treatment to survivorship
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Breast cancer is the most commonly occurring cancer among women globally, while cardiovascular disease has become a leading cause of death among breast cancer survivors. Commonly used breast cancer treatments are known to cause cardiotoxicity, but the majority of studies have not examined the growing population of young and emerging adult breast cancer patients. The objective of this study is to develop a personalized model of psychosocial factors, traditional CVD risk factors, and molecular markers that predicts risk of cardiotoxicity among YEA breast cancer patients.

title: Synthetic Biology Approach in Engineered Algae for Improved Milk Fat Composition in Formula
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative Human milk fat, which provides over 50% of an infant’s energy requirement during early lactation, has an optimized composition with respect to fatty acid chain length and regiospecific distribution. The fat ingredient of infant formula, which is typically derived from vegetable oil blends, does not match the structural diversity of human milk lipids with respect to chemical composition, and hence lacks some significant nutritional benefits of human milk fat. This project aims to provide a healthy and sustainable lipid source for infant formula by engineering an oleaginous green alga for human milk fat production through a synthetic biology approach that exploits the vast knowledge base of plant and algal lipid metabolism.

application_id: 10831050
activity_code: K01
title: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy and Subsequent Risk of Vascular Dementia, Alzheimer's Disease, or Related Dementia: A Retrospective Cohort Study Taking into Account Mid-Life Mediating Factors.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Women have a two-fold higher lifetime risk for Alzheimer Disease (AD), vascular dementia, and related dementias (RD) compared to men. Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, including preeclampsia, eclampsia, and gestational hypertension, may contribute to sex differences in ADRD, with midlife experiences such as depression influencing the magnitude of risk. We propose to 1) create a model that can identify AD and related dementias in a large health administrative database; and 2) test the link (and mediating factors) between hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and ADRD, which will serve as the first step towards creating women’s tailored intervention that can mitigate ADRD risk for women.

title: Roles of autophagy in microglial senescence and Parkinson's disease
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative The greatest risk factor for developing Parkinson’s disease, outweighing all others, is age. However, it is unclear how aging predisposes the brain to PD. This project studies the mechanism of how microglia become senescent, characterizes the senescent microglia, and determines the impact of senescent microglia on dopaminergic neurons using human Parkinson’s disease postmortem brains and human microglia derived from induced pluripotent stem cells.

title: Examining vertical alignment in perceived implementation climate within a trial of motivational interviewing in substance use disorder treatment clinics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Motivational interviewing (MI) is a leading evidence-based practice (EBP) for substance use disorder, but MI use and fidelity have been low in usual care. Strategies have been developed to support program leaders in improving EBP implementation climate, but it is not yet known whether these strategies work by improving alignment in perceived implementation climate between providers and leaders. The proposed project will leverage data from a cluster randomized trial of an organizational implementation strategy (LOCI) to: (1) measure vertical alignment of perceived implementation climate, (2) identify organizational factors associated with alignment, and (3) examine sensitivity to change, and explore associations with exposure to LOCI and provider MI implementation outcomes...

activity_code: F30
title: Pan-cancer genomic characterization of human papillomavirus associated tumors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TENNESSEE HEALTH SCI CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human papilloma virus (HPV) infection accounts for nearly all cervical and anal squamous cell carcinomas, a subset of other anogenital squamous carcinomas, and increasing rates of head and neck squamous cell carcinomas – all with relatively heterogenous clinical outcomes due to a lack of personalized care. application_id: 10834752
activity_code: F31
title: Combining repetitive element-specific T cells with epigenetic therapy to treat ovarian cancer
org_name: GEORGE WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The five year survival rate of ovarian cancer patients has remained at 47% for decades, highlighting the urgent need for therapeutic strategies that activate potent anti-tumor immune responses. One such strategy is epigenetic therapy, which stimulates an immunogenic response in cancer cells by inducing the transcription of repetitive elements (REs)—genomic regions that are normally silent. The goal of this project is to investigate REs as an inducible source of tumor-associated antigens that can be directly targeted by RE-specific T cells in combination with epigenetic therapy, resulting in an increased anti-tumor immune response and potentially curative immune therapy for ovarian cancer patients.

application_id: 10837643
activity_code: F30
title: PBRM1 bromodomain missense mutations in ccRCC vascular signaling
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) is a type of cancer characterized by tumors whose growth relies on making new blood vessels, and therefore, ccRCC responds well to drugs that prevent new blood vessels from being made. ccRCC-associated mutations in a DNA-binding protein called PBRM1 improve patient response to these drugs that prevent tumor blood vessel formation, but mutations that do not inhibit PBRM1 activity are common, and the effects of these mutations on patient drug response are unknown. We will determine how mutations that do not disrupt PBRM1 activity affect how ccRCC tumors increase their blood supply so that patients who will not respond are not unnecessarily given drugs with toxic side effects.

title: Minimizing Hypoglycemia in Adults with Type 1 Diabetes through an Integrated Mobile Health and Continuous Glucose Monitoring System
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative About 1.6 million people in the U.S. have type 1 diabetes, who face the daily threat of developing dangerously low blood glucose episodes, potentially resulting in confusion, life-threatening cardiac events, or even death. Despite using advanced diabetes technologies such as continuous glucose monitors, many patients continue to develop these devastating episodes due to sub-optimal management of low blood glucose events. This research project will expand knowledge of type 1 diabetes patients’ beliefs that prevent them from treating low blood glucose episodes; resultant insight will be applied to develop and pilot test a patient-centered behavior- changing program to improve patients’ self-management of low blood glucose episodes.

org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Cocaine use disorders (CUD) are consistently associated with disrupted processing of non-drug stimuli to dysregulate behavior outside the context of cocaine seeking. Deficits in fundamental behavioral functions such as learning and decision making negatively affect the lives of individuals suffering from CUD, and the severity of these symptoms is strongly associated with disease progression and treatment outcomes. CUDs are at a unique disadvantage when it comes to treatment with no FDA approved therapies, necessitating further research on stimulant use neural dysregulation to produce novel interventions.

phr: Project Narrative The Smc5/6 complex is a conserved protein complex whose roles in genome replication, DNA repair, and suppression of extra-chromosomal DNA underlie its clinical functions of tumor suppression and repression of tumor-causing viruses. To address the mechanistic knowledge gap of how Smc5/6 operates as a molecular machine, this proposal aims to: (1) characterize Smc5/6’s ability to manipulate and compact DNA; (2) elucidate the binding preferences of Smc5/6 on DNA substrates of different topologies; and (3) define Smc5/6’s interactions with DNA replication factors. title: The Role of Dopamine in Cognitive Resilience to Alzheimer's Disease Pathology in Healthy Older Adults
org_name: BRANDEIS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A substantial proportion of the population maintains stable cognitive function despite the development of Alzheimer’s disease (AD)-related pathology. The neural mechanisms underlying this cognitive resilience are poorly understood, though an understanding of these mechanisms has immense therapeutic potential. The proposed research will investigate the independent and interactive effects of one candidate mechanism – compensatory elevation of dopamine synthesis – and AD-related pathology on memory performance and local functional connectivity using a multi-modal neuroimaging approach in aging.

The proposed program is designed to provide training in genetics research to predoctoral students and to prepare them for leadership in scientific research and research-related careers.

application_id: 10848185
activity_code: T32
title: Predoctoral Training at the Interface Chemistry and Biology
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The Chemistry/Biology Interface Training Program (CBITP) at Yale University will provide rigorous, cutting- edge training in chemical biology research to prepare early-career scientists for research and research-related careers in academia, industry, and the non-profit sector. The CBITP is organized around the belief that the rigorous training of diverse individuals with the brightest minds and a deep commitment to probing basic biological mechanisms using chemical approaches will lead to: 1) new discoveries that could open completely new fields; 2) a unique multidisciplinary workforce; and 3) new economic opportunities in the biotech/biopharma space.

activity_code: K01
title: Advancing Alzheimer's Disease Communication and Recruitment Science among American Indians and Alaska Natives
org_name: WASHINGTON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE American Indians and Alaska Natives rarely participate in research studies on Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias. This is unlikely to change unless effective communication and recruitment strategies for clinical studies are developed. In this research, we assess how American Indians and Alaska Natives seek, process and react to online information sources about Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias, develop a culturally tailored website, and administer a pilot study to evaluate the feasibility of a future effectiveness trial.

By virtue of being human cell derived and having a complex multi-layered 3D architecture, human brain organoids are distinct from models traditionally utilized in biomedical research, and have the potential to make an immediate impact on disease treatment. phr: Project Narrative The comprehensive Women’s Reproductive Health Research at Stanford offers OB/GYN junior clinician- investigators an individually tailored program of didactic research methods and mentored career development to bridge the transition to academic independence. application_id: 10851990
activity_code: K01
title: Machine Learning to Reduce Hypertension Treatment Clinical Inertia
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: The objective of the major study proposed in this K01 application is to first, develop and validate a random forest machine learning algorithm (MLA) to predict risk of uncontrolled blood pressure among adults with hypertension. Second, identify barriers and facilitators to implementing an MLA-clinical decision support (CDS) tool that predicts uncontrolled blood pressure into clinical settings by conducting key-informant interviews and focus groups with primary care clinicians and patients. Third, compare clinical management of hypertension with and without information from the MLA on whether a patient will have uncontrolled blood pressure through a case-vignette study.

title: Clearing the Fog: Characterization of the Phenotypic and Neurophysiological Features of Cognitive Dysfunction in Fibromyalgia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative The proposed project will characterize the phenotypic nature of cognitive dysfunction in fibromyalgia (FM) using a combination of self-report and objective measures of cognition, complemented by neurophysiological (EEG) and functional magnetic resonance (fMRI) outcomes. Cognitive symptoms are one of the most debilitating aspects of FM, are poorly understood and thus poorly managed, and are present in a wide range of chronic pain conditions beyond FM. Therefore, this project addresses a critical practical challenge that has applications for the improved healthcare management of a broad range of patients suffering from chronic pain.

title: Cancer-associated fibroblasts promote thyroid cancer malignancy through Wnt signaling
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are a heterogeneous stromal cell population that influence disease course and treatment responses in aggressive cancers. activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing the biochemistry and dynamics of the immune suppressive CXCL12 coat in pancreatic cancer
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Project Narrative While pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is resistant to immune checkpoint blockade (ICB), preventing the formation of the T cell sequestering CXCL12 “coat” renders mouse models of this disease susceptible. This proposal will identify the amino acids of KRT19 required to form the covalent isopeptide bond between it and CXCL12, quantify the rate of CXCL12 coat formation and disassembly, and determine how these processes polarize the immune system. The knowledge gained will guide future pre-clinical and clinical studies attempting to block the formation of the CXCL12 coat in the treatment of PDAC.

application_id: 10863453
activity_code: K23
title: Genetic and MRI biomarkers of neuroplasticity predict aphasia recovery and phenotypes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative Patients with stroke frequently suffer from aphasia, a disorder of expressive and/or receptive language, that can lead to serious health consequences, including social isolation, depression, reduced quality of life, and increased caregiver burden. Aphasia recovery varies greatly between individuals, and likely relies upon the capacity for neuroplasticity, both at a systems level of reorganized brain networks and a molecular level of neuronal repair and plasticity. The proposed work will evaluate genetic and neural network biological markers of neuroplasticity associated with variability in aphasia, with a future goal to improve prognostics and identify therapeutic targets to reduce the long-term burdens of aphasia.

title: A Multi-Method Study of Momentary Distress Intolerance and Combustible Cigarette Smoking
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Distress intolerance (DI), or one’s inability to withstand difficult experiences, physically and emotionally, is a risk factor believed to underlie the co-occurrence of cigarette smoking and emotional distress/disorders. In this mentored research development application, we explore DI as a dynamic variable that can change moment-to- moment as the result of various internal and external factors. Through the proposed multi-method study, our goal is to understand these factors that influence DI and subsequent smoking in daily life in order to inform future interventions that can help people reduce DI in the moments when they are most likely to smoke.

activity_code: K23
title: Enhancing buprenorphine low dose initiation (COPILOT)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The surge in overdose deaths in the United States, primarily due to non-medical fentanyl use, has prompted the exploration of alternative strategies to increase the uptake of buprenorphine, a highly effective medication for opioid use disorder (OUD). Low Dose Initiation (LDI) protocols have emerged as a promising approach, gradually introducing small amounts of buprenorphine to minimize the risk of precipitated withdrawal, though successful initiation using LDI protocols in the outpatient setting remains low. This research proposal aims to address knowledge gaps and enhance LDI protocols through the development of an Enhanced LDI program, with the goal of increasing buprenorphine uptake among individuals with OUD using fentanyl and reducing overdose deaths.

application_id: 10865114
activity_code: K08
title: Developing a Zebrafish Model of Slc6a1/GAT1 Hypofunction and an In Vitro Assay to Identify Novel Treatments
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The molecular mechanisms of epilepsy remain elusive, but genetic epilepsy disorders can provide new insights. Mutations in SLC6A1 also known as GAT1 (GABA transporter 1) cause myoclonic astatic epilepsy, and deficiency of GAT1 function may be implicated in absence epilepsy, but how hypofunction of a GABA transporter leads to epilepsy and how to optimally treat the disorder remains unclear. The current proposal will close these gaps by establishing a novel zebrafish model of GAT1 hypofunction using genetic and pharmacologic approaches to address questions related to the pathogenesis of the disorder and by testing whether a novel in vitro fluorescence-mediated cell-based assay of GAT1 function can be used to identify positive modulators with therapeutic value.

title: Low-cost integrated nucleic acid amplification testing platform for cervical cancer screening in low- and middle-income countries
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cervical cancer is a life-threatening pathology highly associated with human papilloma virus (HPV) infection that disproportionally affects low- and middle- income countries. A major barrier in implementing and sustaining screening programs for cervical cancer in low-resource countries is the lack of an affordable and effective screening test that can provide HPV results at the point-of-care. This proposal will develop and evaluate a novel DNA-based approach that combines isothermal recombinase polymerase amplification with fluorescent quantification in an accurate and low-cost test, which could improve access to cervical cancer screening and significantly decrease mortality due to the disease.

application_id: 10868742
activity_code: F32
title: Deteriming the Role of Caspase Cleaved Tau in Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Patients with Alzheimer’s disease have plaques in their brains containing, among other things, caspase cleaved tau protein. The goal of this proposal is to characterize the role that caspase cleavage of tau plays in the progression of tau pathology. The outcome of these experiments will provide valuable insight into events happening early in the progression of Alzheimer’s disease as well as further validate caspases as targets for the development of treatments for the disease.

title: Revealing roles of heterosynaptic plasticity in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia and is characterized by a progressive cognitive decline and neuronal dysfunction. activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the divergent functions of mutant p53
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The TP53 gene is mutated in about half of all cancers, with strong selection for missense mutations in the DNA binding domain. The resulting missense mutant protein can have different functions that affect various aspects of tumor formation and progression, such as the ability to metastasize, yet it has been extraordinarily difficult to sort out to what extent these functions are conserved between mutants. Systematically profiling the transcriptome of cells that harbor mutant p53 proteins, coupled with a detailed mechanistic characterization of representative mutants from each class, can potentially inform treatment strategies for cancer patients harboring tumors driven by specific TP53 alleles.

activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating mechanisms of therapy response in BRCA2 mutant prostate cancers
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPis) were recently approved to treat advanced BRCA2 mutant prostate cancers, yet little is known about the molecular response of these tumors to this therapy. This proposal employs novel model systems of BRCA2 mutant prostate cancer to explore the molecular alterations that occur in response to PARPi therapy, with specific focus on extracellular matrix (ECM) pathways identified in preliminary results. The proposed studies will interrogate the impact of PARPi on ECM modulation and examine the contribution of the ECM gene SERPINE1, with the long-term goal of utilizing the knowledge gleaned to develop novel targeted therapies.

title: Brain, Immunology and Glia Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE For decades, the immune system and central nervous system were thought to operate independently one of another. The Brain Immunology and Glia Training Program will provide a rich interdisciplinary training environment that leads to scientific breakthroughs and provides comprehensive training that will prepare trainees for careers in neuroimmunology and glia research.

activity_code: F30
title: Alternate splicing as a source of shared neoantigens in a non-small cell lung cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT SCH OF MED/DNT
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alternative splicing is a post-transcriptional regulatory process that is dysregulated in cancer and may generate unique peptides, called neoantigens, that can be recognized by antitumor CD8+ T cells. We have built a bioinformatic pipeline that has identified 21 candidate alternative splicing-derived neoantigens in non-small cell lung cancer, a deadly cancer type. The experiments outlined in this proposal will determine how these molecules contribute to the immunogenicity of non-small cell lung cancer, which may facilitate the development of new immunotherapies for this devastating disease.

activity_code: F31
title: Function of NLRP3 in regulatory T cell-mediated control of intestinal homeostasis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative The inflammasome component NLRP3 is well known for its function in innate immunity, but its unexpected expression in T cells has sparked an exploration of its role in adaptive immunity. By taking both loss-of-function and gain-of-function approaches, this study will help gain mechanistic insights into the underappreciated anti- inflammatory role of NLRP3 in intestinal Tregs. The results obtained from this study will undoubtedly guide effective therapies for chronic intestinal disorders such as inflammatory bowel disease, in which the anti- inflammatory vs. proinflammatory role of NLRP3 is still debated.

application_id: 10885454
activity_code: K23
title: Identifying sleep-related risk factors for PTSD and their underlying mechanisms
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Sleep disturbances (difficulties falling or staying asleep) are common within the first month after a traumatic event and are a significant risk factor that contributes to the development of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). This study will identify individuals with a trait susceptibility to posttraumatic sleep disturbances and determine the mechanisms by which these sleep disturbances increase risk of PTSD. These goals are critical to 1) identify high-risk groups toward whom preventative treatments can be targeted, and 2) inform the development of sleep-focused treatments that help lower risk of PTSD.

title: Activation of Synovial Lining Fibroblasts in Rheumatoid Arthritis
org_name: HOSPITAL FOR SPECIAL SURGERY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In rheumatoid arthritis, the synovial lining is hyperplastic and primarily composed of activated fibroblasts that secrete multiple factors implicated in tissue damage. In this proposal we investigate mechanisms by which these fibroblasts are activated by components of the inflammatory microenvironment, including cytokines from infiltrating leukocytes, and how this activation results in their functional specialization. The identification of key, functionally relevant transcription factors and pathways will facilitate the development of fibroblast-directed therapeutics.

title: Gendered Racism Profiles, Resilience, and High-Risk Pregnancies Among Black Women
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Black women in the United States develop high-risk pregnancy conditions such gestational diabetes and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy at much higher rates than White or Latina women, which increases their risk for poor outcomes during pregnancy and after birth. title: Encoding of fear and safety discrimination in prefrontal-amygdala projections
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Fear is an essential adaptive mechanism for interacting with a changing environment, but fear can be maladaptive when it overgeneralizes, leading to distressing and intrusive defensive reactions in objectively safe situations. This is a common feature in anxiety and trauma-related disorders, for which current treatments provide at most minimal symptom relief in a third of affected patients. This research proposal will identify the neural circuit activity underlying fear discrimination, with the long-term goal of advancing the development of new treatments for anxiety and trauma-related disorders.

application_id: 10886074
activity_code: K23
title: Systemic Mediators and Biomarkers of Injury in Preterm Infants with Intermittent Hypoxemia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Preterm infants (~0.4 million born in the US yearly) are at risk for frequent blood oxygen drops (Intermittent Hypoxemia (IH)) that are linked to multiple short- and long-term neonatal morbidities leading to both lifelong impairments and increased burden on health care cost. Our study investigates both markers and mechanisms of brain injury from increased intermittent hypoxemia in preterm infants. These data will contribute to our understanding of brain injury from intermittent hypoxemia and identify potential targets for developing treatments in the future to improve IH and associated impairments.

application_id: 10886142
activity_code: F31
title: Targeting cellular senescence with oral fisetin supplementation to improve vascular aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The risk of developing cardiovascular diseases increases with aging largely due to declines in the function of arteries which are influenced by cellular senescence, a state of nearly irreversible cell cycle arrest. This study will investigate the potential of a novel, natural dietary supplement, fisetin, for treating age-related arterial dysfunction by suppressing cellular senescence. Overall, this research has the potential to identify a new therapeutic strategy – with great promise as a dietary supplement and for the translation to humans for treating and preventing age-related arterial dysfunction and thereby reducing risk of cardiovascular diseases.

application_id: 10886147
activity_code: K01
title: Neural Network Approach to Estimate Fetal Weight in the Late Third Trimester of Pregnancy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Fetal weight estimation, or the assessment of antenatal fetal weight for the purposes of growth tracking, labor planning, and management, is a critical component of safe prenatal care. Estimation of fetal weight, typically accomplished using ultrasound-derived measurements, is often inaccurate with up to 15% error for newborns of anticipated normal weight and up to 40% for newborns above 4,000 grams at birth. Our goal is to develop an image analysis algorithm to more accurately estimate fetal weight in the late third trimester of pregnancy.

application_id: 10886393
activity_code: K99
title: Spinal Direct Current Stimulation to Enhance Breathing Following Cervical Spinal Cord Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cervical spinal cord injury induced respiratory complications are a leading cause of mortality and morbidity in this patient population, underscoring a critically unmet need. The fundamental goal of this proposal is to employ a combinatorial approach (transcutaneous spinal direct current stimulation in conjunction with ampakine pharmacotherapy) to enhance respiratory function after a cervical spinal cord injury. These studies will directly test whether this combinatorial therapy can be used as a safe and neuroplasticity-inducing rehabilitation modality for eventual translation into humans with incomplete chronic spinal injury, all the while, setting a foundation for the applicant (Dr. Rana) to launch an independent research career.

title: Targeting pyrimidine biosynthesis in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a devastating disease with a five-year survival rate of less than 10 percent, largely due to a lack of any effective therapy. Numerous studies have implicated pyrimidine nucleotide synthesis as a critical metabolic pathway for PDAC cell proliferation and viability; however, inhibitors of DHODH, a key enzyme in this pathway, have consistently failed to show efficacy as monotherapy in various cancer settings. These studies will build off our preliminary discovery of a pre-treatment myeloid-associated gene signature predictive of ICB resistance in bladder cancer by creating an immune atlas of treatment-naïve bladder cancer tumor, peripheral blood, and urine, a resource that does not currently exist, and establishing an in vitro monocyte- macrophage culture system to define and modulate the precise biological pathways and myeloid subsets underlying ICB resistance. activity_code: K23
title: Neural, genetic, and environmental indicators of treatment outcomes for adolescent substance use
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While substance problems and associated externalizing behaviors (i.e., violence, delinquency) have a significant negative impact on health and development, empirically-supported treatments are time- and resource-intensive, placing a high burden on youth and families engaged in treatment as well as the therapists providing these services. The training and research described in this proposal will examine how neural, genetic, and environmental risk factors for early substance use problems are modified by and associated with Multisystemic Therapy treatment outcomes. This project has the potential to establish novel approaches to evaluating the impact of targeted treatment on mechanisms that contribute to severe and persistent substance use, with the long-term goal of improvin...

application_id: 10887413
activity_code: F30
title: Extracellular Matrix Vesicles and Intercellular Communication of Osteoblast-lineage Cells
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Matrix vesicles, small extracellular vesicles released by osteoblasts and anchored in the extracellular matrix, are enriched with microRNA and other cell signaling molecules that may mediate bone development and regeneration. The role and mechanism of action of these matrix vesicles in mature bone remains to be elucidated, but may translate into therapeutic potential for orthopedic pathologies, including critical size bone defects and chronic nonunions. This study investigates the physical and chemical characteristics of matrix vesicles and their function with regard to cellular signaling pathway activation and new bone formation, with the ultimate goal of a novel therapeutic application to improve bone healing.

application_id: 10887478
activity_code: K23
title: Cellular and Physical Function Outcomes Leading to Failed Muscle Recovery After Critical Illness
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: Project Narrative Patients surviving critical illness acquire impairments in muscle and physical function that can persist for years after hospital discharge. The planned study will identify cellular and physical function outcomes that contribute to failed muscle regrowth in the recovery phase of critical illness. My training will entail statistical analyses for basic and applied science and the experiments to assess human skeletal muscle to determine cellular mechanisms that drive impairments in muscle and contribute to sustained physical functional deficits.

application_id: 10890152
activity_code: K01
title: Developing a Digital Intervention for Adolescent Nonsuicidal Self-injury
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims to develop and evaluate a digital mental health intervention (DMHI) for adolescents who engage in nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) and who are not currently engaged in treatment. I will employ user- centered design methodologies to understand adolescents' specific needs and preferences, which will guide the development of a scalable, low-intensity DMHI designed to be implemented within the digital offerings of a large mental health advocacy website. The DMHI will support the acquisition and practice of skills to reduce NSSI frequency, and encourage use of external supports to increase further help-seeking among high-risk adolescents who are currently unable or uninterested in accessing traditional psychological treatments.

application_id: 10891423
activity_code: F30
title: Endogenous memory T cell mediated rejection of high-risk cardiac allografts
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute allograft rejection is a T cell mediated immune response that continues to undermine heart transplant outcomes. Endogenous (pre-existing) memory T cells infiltrate cardiac allografts early after graft reperfusion and the proposed experiments will test the mechanisms by which these memory T cells are activated to mediate increased graft tissue injury and rejection of allografts subjected to prolonged cold ischemic storage. Findings from these studies should reveal new therapeutic targets to dampen early post-transplant inflammation and inhibit endogenous memory CD8 T cell-mediated acute graft injury and rejection of high-risk allografts in unsensitized recipients.

application_id: 10893353
activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing and Targeting ERBB2 Mutations in Invasive Lobular Carcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Activating ERBB2 mutations are enriched in invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) of the breast, suggesting a crosstalk between ERBB2 mutations and the characteristic loss of CDH1 in ILC. Clinical trials have demonstrated promising efficacy using anti-HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitors in patients with ERBB2 mutant ILC. The ultimate goal of the proposed studies is to characterize the functional roles of recurrent ERBB2 mutations in ILC vs IDC using innovative preclinical models and provide rationale for specific combination targeted therapy in patients with ERBB2 mutant ILC, in order to enhance anti-tumor activity and/or delay drug resistance.

application_id: 10894002
activity_code: K23
title: Role of FODMAPs in the pathophysiology of diarrhea-predominant Irritable bowel syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: NARRATIVE Diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D) is a chronic disease of the gastrointestinal tract, which affects about 2.1-4.5% of the United States population. A diet high in fermentable oligo-, di- and mono- saccharides exacerbates IBS-D symptoms while a diet low in FODMAPs provides some symptomatic relief in ~50-60% of these patients. The proposed research will advance our understanding of the role of FODMAPs in the pathophysiology of IBS allowing for more effective, individualized treatment for patients with IBS-D, which in turn will improve patient satisfaction with treatment and reduce healthcare costs.

title: Hormones and Genes in Women's Health: From Bench to Bedside
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although there are significant sex differences in the expression and onset of major medical and psychiatric disorders, clinical decisions based on research are primarily based on data from men and may often be inappropriate for women. Thus, there remains a strong need for the Harvard BIRCWH program to educate, train, and develop junior investigators who will commit their careers to studying the impact of sex and gender on clinical disorders. Our Harvard BIRCWH program will provide the basis for the development of sex- dependent treatment approaches and public awareness of the importance of these sex-dependent health issues for families and society.

application_id: 10894327
activity_code: F31
title: Quantitative Magnetic Resonance Properties of Cerebrospinal Fluid and Brain Health Outcomes in Pediatric Congenital Heart Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Children with congenital heart diseases (CHD) often demonstrate neurodevelopmental deficits – that manifest as difficulties with language, cognitive tasks, and impaired executive function – and the risk factors that may contribute to this poor outcome remains elusive. Based on prior studies, altered CSF volumes and perturbation to CSF circulation may play a role in poor neurodevelopmental outcomes in CHD. This study aims to establish the relationship between abnormalities in CSF, as quantified using multi-modal magnetic resonance imaging techniques and neurodevelopmental deficits in children and adolescents with CHD.

application_id: 10896898
activity_code: F30
title: Fentanyl Addiction: Individual Differences, Neural Circuitry, and Treatment with a GLP-1 Receptor Agonist
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIV HERSHEY MED CTR
phr: Project Narrative Opioid use disorder (OUD) ruins the lives of those with the disorder and those around them, while also placing higher healthcare and economic burden on the United States. Here we will use drug self-administration and light sheet microscopy to identify the impact of high vs. low fentanyl seeking on brain in male and female rats and we will test the ‘need-state’ hypothesis by determining whether treatment with a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1RA) not only rescues vulnerable rats from fentanyl seeking but also reengages the GLP-1 ‘satiety’ pathway and disengages substrates involved in reward and seeking.

title: Developing and Testing an Opioid Taper Intervention Before Total Knee Arthroplasty
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic preoperative opioid use is associated with worse surgical outcomes in total knee arthroplasty. Tapering opioids preoperatively holds potential to improve outcomes for these patients, but high-quality evidence of efficacy is lacking, and the best approach to taper opioids in this setting is not known. This application will support an early-career investigator to develop and begin testing a preoperative opioid tapering intervention, which has the long-term potential to improve care in this very common operations.

application_id: 10897029
activity_code: K23
title: Validation of a Novel Ultrasound Score to Improve the Assessment of Joint Inflammation in Children with Juvenile Arthritis
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are many challenges when determining the presence and severity of joint inflammation by physical examination in children with Juvenile Arthritis. This research will 1) determine if a novel 10-joint musculoskeletal ultrasound score effectively assesses joint inflammation at the bedside, and 2) investigate the relationship of the 10-joint musculoskeletal ultrasound score with serum and synovial biomarkers. It is anticipated that accurate detection of joint inflammation may support safer and more appropriate choices for the treatment of arthritis, which in turn will improve the outcomes and quality of life of children with Juvenile Arthritis.

application_id: 10897255
activity_code: K23
title: Improving Prehospital Pediatric Emergency Readiness with Telemedicine
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our objective is to determine if video-conferencing with pediatric experts using mobile devices (teleconsultation) can improve emergency care for sick and injured children in ambulances. With emergency service personnel, we will: (1) optimize a video-conferencing platform for efficient and easy use outside the hospital; (2) determine whether they would and could use this technology, and if it makes care safer using infant patient simulators; and (3) determine if pediatric expert physicians in hospitals can use this technology to assess children in ambulances before they arrive at the hospital. This work has the potential to improve access to high quality emergency care for children, especially in rural and underserved communities.

application_id: 10897283
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular computation by the CaMKII holoenzyme
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE CaMKII is an extremely abundant neuronal protein and a central mediator of the strengthening and weakening of neuronal synapses, termed bidirectional synaptic plasticity. This proposal will determine how the unique three- dimensional structure of CaMKII enables its dual role in these two opposing plasticity modes. The results from these experiments will provide critical insight into the normal role of CaMKII in synaptic plasticity and will in turn inform studies of CaMKII dysregulation in models of neurodegenerative, neurological, and neuropsychiatric disease.

application_id: 10897796
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of TET2-dependent control of CAR T cell fate determination and antitumor function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells have been a breakthrough in personalized cancer treatment; however, this therapy is not effective for many patients. Preliminary findings suggest that the epigenetic modifier TET2 is involved in controlling T cell fate and function; thus, I propose to investigate TET2 to identify strategies to improve CART cell efficacy in response to tumor challenge. By investigating the mechanisms and extent to which TET2 modulates antitumor T cell function, this research has the potential to improve patient responses to CAR T cell therapy as well as illuminate more general mechanisms by which DNA modifying enzymes alter the epigenetic landscape to regulate gene expression.

title: Immunoproteomic mechanisms of human macrophage resistance to Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE We have identified unique macrophage responses in humans who naturally resist Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) infection despite heavy exposure. The aims of this proposal are to uncover proteomic determinants of this macrophage resistance and define the underlying cellular mechanisms and Mtb-host protein interactions. Once key protective pathways are identified, they can be augmented with targeted host-directed therapies that improve killing of drug-sensitive and drug-resistant Mtb.

application_id: 10898005
activity_code: K23
title: Predictors of Low-risk Phenotypes after Traumatic Brain Injury Incorporating Proteomic Biomarker Signatures.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a leading cause of death in the US, and treatment options are limited—we lack methods to assess a patient’s risk of deterioration at admission. An improved ability to classify patients early after injury will allow us to identify those who may benefit from aggressive interventions versus those who can safely be observed. The goal of the proposed project is to develop and assess a series of predictive models incorporating immune proteins from peripheral blood to aid in the prediction of which patients are at low-risk for complications and poor outcome after TBI.

application_id: 10899915
activity_code: F31
title: Non-Classical Ion Channel Function in Human Cortical Histogenesis
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative: The purpose of membrane depolarization and ionic flux in human cortical histogenesis remains poorly examined. The recent identification by our lab of mutations in the gene encoding the ATP-releasing ion channel Pannexin 1 (PANX1) associated with cases of polymicrogyria (many small brain folds; PMG) provides a window for investigating processes of brain development regulated by electrical coupling. The proposed research aims to functionally investigate these novel PANX1 variants to further both our understanding of the gene’s role in PMG pathogenesis, as well as the impact of early electrical activity on developmental processes such as neural proliferation, migration, and differentiation.
