title: Developing an Accurate and Robust Targeting Technique to Enable Transcostal Histotripsy Treatment of Liver Tumors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Histotripsy is an emerging noninvasive, nonthermal, and nonionizing tumor treatment that uses focused ultrasound to mechanically destroy tissue. title: The Role of Parabrachial CGRP Neurons in Opioid Reinforcement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This study seeks to address the growing opioid epidemic by identifying novel therapeutic targets for treating opioid use disorder. application_id: 10903359
activity_code: F31
title: Human Cortical Ripples as a Mechanism for Neural Binding
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Binding Problem asks how the diverse contents of awareness are unified, a cortical process that is fundamental to understanding healthy and disrupted cognition in humans. This study will test if bursts of high- frequency cortical oscillations (i.e., ‘ripples’) possess the properties crucial to a possible role in binding. If successful, this work will lay the foundation to study neural binding in the human cortex, thereby facilitating the investigation of how information processing is disrupted in many form of neuropsychiatric disease.

application_id: 10903744
activity_code: F31
title: Characterizing the Sensorimotor Transformation in Drosophila olfactory system
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The goal of this proposal is to characterize how the neural signals encoding odor information for a biologically relevant segment of the fly olfactory system are transformed at critical points within this system. In doing so, we hope to identify how these signal transformations can be associated with changes in specific aspects of fly behavior, like whether they turn more or slow down when making sharp turns. By studying how these signals are transformed and how these transformations impact behavior, we hope to gain a better understanding of how the fly olfactory system influences the selection of specific motor programs during ongoing locomotion.

activity_code: F30
title: Understanding the role of β2AR-signaling on ILC2 differentiation/plasticity and its implications in cancer progression
org_name: ROSWELL PARK CANCER INSTITUTE CORP
phr: Project Narrative Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are highly plastic cells that can exert both enhancing and regulatory activity in tumor microenvironment depending on the type of signals they receive. Our hypothesis is that the suppressor functions of ILC2s are enhanced by stress through β2 adrenergic signaling and thus contributes to the known tumor promoting effects of stress. The work proposed here will investigate how adrenergic stress affects ILC2 plasticity and immune-inhibitory functionality in the tumor site with the long-term intent of improving outcomes of immunotherapy in patients.

title: Use of Machine Learning on Integrated Electronic Medical Record, Genetic andWaveform Data to Predict Perioperative Cardiorespiratory Instability
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative During the perioperative period hemodynamic instability is the norm rather than the exception; the ability of clinicians to manage this instability has been associated with postoperative complications affecting more than 15 million Americans and costing more than $165 billion each year. There are no current risk scores that predict instability in real time, however the recent widespread adoption of electronic medical records and creation of genomic biobanks creates unique opportunities to develop scores that are both accurate and precise. We will use machine learning techniques to create risk scores for perioperative cardiorespiratory instability – specifically hypotension and arrhythmia – using combined electronic medical record, genomic and physiologic waveform data.

activity_code: K01
title: Role of the prorenin receptor in blood pressure and autonomic control through the local activation of the renin angiotensin system in the brainstem
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE We have identified a cell population expressing renin within the rostral ventrolateral medulla (RVLM), a brainstem region involved in sympathetic control. The goal of this proposal is to elucidate the requirement of the prorenin receptor for the non-hydrolytic activation of prorenin within the RVLM. We also hypothesize that conditional ablation of prorenin receptor in the RVLM attenuates the manifestation of the cardiovascular abnormalities due to brain renin angiotensin system activation.

application_id: 10908701
activity_code: K23
title: Altered High-Density Lipoprotein function in Patients with Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Damage to the vascular endothelium is not only responsible for cardiovascular disease – the leading cause of mortality in patients with autoimmune diseases – but also implicated in the pathogenesis of idiopathic inflammatory myopathies (IIM). This study will investigate the possible link between vascular damage and dysfunctional pro-inflammatory HDL, which is known to promote lipid oxidation and perpetuate endothelial damage, in a longitudinal cohort of patients with IIM. Understanding how HDL function contributes to not only macrovascular damage but also microvascular damage in IIM could offer promise for emerging therapeutic targets to improve the lives of patients with IIM as well as other autoimmune diseases.

title: Emotion processing, gender identity, and differential risk for anxiety and depression in autistic adults
org_name: GEORGE MASON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The goal of the proposed research is to illuminate cognitive and neural factors associated with the elevated risk for co-occurring anxiety and depression in young adults (age 18-30 years) with autism spectrum disorder [ASD]. Using structural and functional brain imaging and measures of emotion processing, understudied populations who are at dramatically elevated risk for anxiety and depression will be examined: women and gender diverse adults. Results will advance our understanding of cognitive and brain mechanisms related to elevated risk for anxiety and depression in ASD and inform interventions that will reduce the burden of these serious mental illnesses in autistic individuals.

phr: Narrative The Minnesota IRACDA Program is a collaboration between the University of Minnesota and the nearby community colleges of Normandale Community College (NCC) and North Hennepin Community College (NHCC), that provides a diverse cohort of postdoctoral scholars in the biomedical sciences with rigorous scientific research training, pedagogical instruction and experience in teaching, mentoring, and career development guidance. activity_code: K01
title: Impact of the Gut Microbiome on Astrocyte Barrier Function
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative: Despite significant advances in multiple sclerosis (MS) therapeutics, current treatments for MS are expensive, complicated to manage and suboptimal in that many patients continue to have breakthrough disease activity and progression, as well as expected adverse effects of medications that suppress the immune system. Disease progression in MS is mediated by activation of astrocyte and microglia, yet none of the currently approved FDA drugs for the treatment of MS effectively target these glial cells. This project aims at identifying gut microbiota and gut microbiota derived metabolites that regulate astrocytes function and their impact on neuroinflammation with the ultimate goal to develop the first microbiome-based therapies for the prevention and treatment of MS.

activity_code: K08
title: O-glycosylation mechanisms of neurological deficits in congenital disorders of glycosylation
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Glycosylation is an essential, post-translational protein modification with complex and poorly understood roles in protein function. This project utilizes a mouse model and glycoproteomics to identify the cellular and molecular origin of neurological dysfunction in a rare disease (GALNT2-CDG) caused by genetic defects in a critical glycosyltransferase protein that initiates the first step in mucin-type O-glycosylation. Understanding glycosylation in the nervous system will elucidate the pathophysiology of rare genetic diseases that disrupt glycosylation (known as congenital disorders of glycosylation) as well as other neurological diseases, enable therapeutic advances targeting glycosylation pathways, and inform normal functions of glycosylation.

title: Genome and Epigenome Integrity In Cancer
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Two fundamental, but mechanistically interdependent, processes that drive cancer development are genetic mutations (genome instability) and epigenome reprogramming (epigenome instability). Leveraging strong support from the Herbert Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center at Columbia University and the expertise of 25 outstanding faculty mentors, we propose to create the first Genome and Epigenome Integrity in Cancer (GEIC) Training Program to foster a group of next generation scientists with diverse backgrounds who will lead in the discovery of mechanisms underlying genome and epigenome instability in cancer and in the translating these discoveries into future therapeutic interventions of cancer.

title: Placental CRH and child cardiovascular disease risk and obesogenic eating behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DENVER (COLORADO SEMINARY)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is the leading cause of death in the US and the origins of CVD begin early in life, however, the mechanisms by which early experiences influence CVD risk are poorly understood. This project examines whether the stress responsive hormone placental corticotrophin-releasing hormone is associated with offspring CVD risk and obesogenic eating behaviors in early childhood. Consistent with NHLBI and American Heart Association goals, these findings could elucidate a potential mechanism by which prenatal factors influence cardiovascular health and provide targets for early childhood prevention of later CVD.

title: Vanderbilt-Mozambique Biomedical Informatics (VM-BMI) Training and Research Program
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE Mozambique (a country in Africa with the fourth highest HIV prevalence globally) has made good progress in HIV pandemic control, but significant gaps remain. phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The current proposal seeks to uncover the population genetic determinants of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), the leading global cause of heart transplantation. title: An investigation of reward brain circuitry structure and function in individuals with co-occurring alcohol use disorder and bipolar disorder and their unaffected offspring
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Nearly 40% of individuals with bipolar disorder (BD) will develop co-occurring alcohol use disorder (AUD+BD), associated with significantly worse clinical course, poorer treatment response, and increased risk for suicide relative to AUD or BD alone. title: Investigating the Role of MBNL1 in Maintaining Cardiomyocyte Terminal Differentiation.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. This project seeks to define how the protein Muscleblind-like protein 1 (MBNL1) regulates cardiomyocyte differentiation and heart regeneration. Heart disease is the number one cause of death globally because the adult heart is incapable of any meaningful regeneration after injury due to a postnatal developmental switch during which cardiomyocytes transition from an immature, proliferative state to a mature, terminally differentiated state largely incapable of proliferation. MBNL1 has never been studied in this context, but understanding its role in controlling cardiomyocyte differentiation, cardiac regeneration, and the mechanisms by which it acts will allow us to determine if modulation of MBNL1 could be used therapeutically for the creation of novel regenerative medici...

title: Investigating and targeting metabolic vulnerabilities of MYC-driven small cell lung cancer
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is the deadliest and most difficult-to-treat subtype of lung cancer with a standard-of-care that has remained largely unchanged for decades. Arginine deprivation by pegylated arginine deiminase (ADI-PEG20) is the most effective targeted therapy in preclinical studies of MYC-driven SCLC, but tumors eventually become resistant to ADI-PEG20. In accordance with NIH’s mission to advance human health, this study will interrogate mechanisms of ADI-PEG20 resistance to identify therapeutic strategies that prolong the efficacy of ADI-PEG20 and improve survival of patients with SCLC.

org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the second leading cause of cancer mortality globally and understanding the microbiota contributions to disease development is predicted to lead to new approaches to CRC prevention and therapy. title: Development of excited-state bond homolysis as a key step for Ni catalysis

application_id: 10918510
activity_code: K23
title: Leveraging mHealth to develop syndemic-based behavioral medicine HIV prevention for the emergency department to reach minoritized PWUD
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite nationally declining rates and efficacious interventions for HIV prevention, services are not reaching minoritized people who use drugs who continue to face high incidence. Leveraging syndemic theory, consultation-liaison psychology, emergency departments, and mHealth, the goal of this project is to develop a low threshold, real-world, multilevel HIV prevention approach. This study is consistent with national Ending the HIV Epidemic goals and NIH priorities for HIV/AIDS research, by aiming to optimize and improve how we serve those at high risk for being missed by and/or falling off the HIV prevention cascade.

activity_code: F31
title: Defining a cross-primed anti-tumor T cell signature to guide immunotherapy development
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cross-priming of CD8+ T cells by conventional Type 1 dendritic cells (cDC1) is necessary for effective antitumor therapy, yet there is no established method for measuring T cell cross- or direct-priming in vivo. This project seeks to define a signature of cross-primed T cells using RNA-seq of tumor-specific T cells isolated from H2Kd-/- tumor-bearing mice that can only cross-prime T cells, or allogeneic mice that can only directly prime T cells via direct tumoral antigen presentation. This will produce cross- and direct-primed T cell signatures that can be used to guide design of immunotherapies by targeting novel checkpoints or costimulatory receptors, to monitor responses to immunotherapy, and to predict responses to checkpoint blockade and similar therapies.

title: Nucleus reuniens, chronic ethanol and cognitive deficits
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chronic alcohol exposure causes cognitive deficits, which are associated with increased relapse probability and continued misuse. I hypothesize that chronic alcohol exposure produces cognitive deficits through altering activity and function of the nucleus reuniens of the thalamus and that normalizing its activity will attenuate cognitive dysfunction. activity_code: K23
title: Development and Implementation of Optimal Rehabilitation Care Pathways to Reduce Disparities in Rehabilitation Outcomes after Joint Replacement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sociodemographic disparities in physical function and use of rehabilitation following total joint arthroplasty have been established, but there is little to no available research regarding approaches to reduce disparities. title: Studying Protein Affinity to DNA by in vitro Transcription and Sequencing (PADIT-seq)
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Genes, which contain instructions for making all proteins in the body, are regulated by sequence-specific DNA interactions by transcription factors (TF). Current methods to detect TF-DNA interactions are biased for only the highest affinity interactions when medium-low affinity interactions are very important and more common. I have developed PADIT-seq, an improved technology to detect TF-DNA interactions across the entire spectrum of affinities. The overall goal of this proposal is to utilize and further develop PADIT-seq to uncover fundamental TF-DNA binding principles, which will significantly advance our mechanistic understanding of the principles governing gene regulation and genetic risk factors for complex diseases.

application_id: 10949537
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding the Role of EIF4A2 in Neurodevelopment
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neurodevelopmental disorders are common, affecting 3% of the global population; these conditions include global developmental delay/intellectual disability, epilepsy, and autism spectrum disorder and are collectively associated with significant healthcare costs for the many children and families affected. Interneuronopathies are becoming more widely recognized as an important cause of neurodevelopmental disorders, with Fragile X and autism both associated with interneuron impairment. This K08 proposal will elucidate both the mechanism and the timeline during which EIF4A2, a DEAD-box encoding gene and novel regulator of interneurons, alters interneuron development, providing direction for future investigations of interneuron-targeted therapeutics.

application_id: 10975774
activity_code: K01
title: Molecular Regulation of a Transcriptionally Poised State in Neurons and its Role in Learning
org_name: LOYOLA UNIVERSITY CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The formation of a new memory requires a rapid increase in the synthesis of messenger RNAs via the process of gene transcription in the brain, and many of the molecular processes underlying gene transcription regulation are disrupted in cognitive disorders. Rapidly inducible gene transcription requires a set of factors that regulate a poised, ready for activation, state within the nucleus, but how this poise is established has not been investigated in neurons. This project will examine the molecular players that regulate the poised state before, during, and after neuronal stimulation to understand whether they influence the capacity of the brain to form memories.

org_name: NEW YORK STATE PSYCHIATRIC INSTITUTE DBA RESEARCH FOUNDATION FOR MENTAL HYGIENE, INC
phr: Project Narrative  Deficits in cognitive behavioral flexibility lead to disability and pose barriers to recovery in many mental disorders, including schizophrenia, autism, post-traumatic stress disorder, and dementias, but these symptoms are largely unaddressed by conventional pharmacologic or somatic therapies. To develop new ways of intervening to improve or even prevent these neurocognitive impairments, we must better understand the underlying brain circuits, their networking, and how they coordinate their activity. title: Genetic Association Between Alzheimer's Disease and Cardio-Cerebrovascular Risk Factors
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cardio and cerebrovascular risk factors (CVRFs) such as hypertension, diabetes, heart disease, obesity, and stroke increase the risk of Alzheimer's disease (AD). This project uses cutting-edge multi-omics data in humans with advanced statistical methods to identify novel genes that interact with CVRFs in AD using GWAS and to identify epigenomic and transcriptomic profiles, and integrative pathways in AD that interacts with vascular pathologies. org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common endocrine condition affecting adult women, with significant comorbidities including diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and infertility. The earliest signs of PCOS appear in peripubertal females, representing an important opportunity for prevention. However, there is limited data on targeting at-risk girls for preventive interventions. This career development award will take a stepwise approach in examining the development of PCOS risk among young females in relation to physical activity behaviors, followed by a structured plan to refine and pilot a targeted physical activity intervention for peripubertal girls.

title: Understanding the role of BORCS5 in neuronal lysosomal function and neurodegeneration
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project narrative A central role for lysosomal dysfunction has been highlighted for neurodegenerative disease mechanisms across multiple genetic forms of movement disorders, including Parkinson disease (PD) and dystonia. The proposed research will investigate the function of BORCS5, a lysosomal gene that we recently identified as a novel genetic cause of neurodegeneration with parkinsonism and dystonia. The project will reveal mechanistic insights into the role of BORCS5 in regulating the complex interplay of lysosomal intracellular dynamics, maturation and degradative properties with the goal of identifying novel therapeutic angles for targeting lysosomal dysfunction in neurodegenerative disorders.

title: Optimization and mechanisms of electrical neurostimulation in mouse models of epilepsy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Drug-resistant epilepsy is a devastating neurological disorder that afflicts approximately 1 million people in the United States who have limited effective treatment options. Brain stimulation is a novel therapy for epilepsy that is adjustable and generally well-tolerated; however, the optimal protocols and underlying mechanisms of neurostimulation in epilepsy remain unknown. The proposed study will explore the temporal and spatial optimization of neurostimulation in epilepsy, and investigate its novel mechanistic effects on disease evolution with the ultimate goal to improve neurostimulation therapy and the lives of people with epilepsy.

application_id: 10994892
activity_code: F30
title: Immunosuppressive impact of the GPCR/MALT1 protease axis in triple-negative breast cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is a subtype of breast cancer that is associated with particularly poor outcomes and chemotherapy has been the mainstay of TNBC treatment for many years. With the recent advent of immunotherapy, certain patients with TNBC have experienced improved outcomes, although most patients fail to respond initially or develop resistance due to an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. This proposal investigates a molecular mechanism by which TNBC cells induce immunosuppression in their tumor microenvironment, paving the way for future development of new immunotherapeutic approaches to improving outcomes for patients with this challenging cancer.

title: Investigating the Mechanism of Hair Follicle Placode Induction : A novel approach to understanding establishment of the periodic pattern

title: Assessing the mechanism by which fetal glucagon inhibits placental nutrient transfer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While hormones of fetal origin have long been postulated to regulate placental nutrient delivery, our laboratory recently produced the first direct evidence of this phenomenon with chronic (9-day) fetal glucagon infusions reducing uterine blood flow, placental nutrient transfer, and placental lactogen secretion. title: Mechanisms of PCDH19-clustering epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE PCDH19-clustering epilepsy (PCE) is a severe developmental and epileptic encephalopathy predominantly affecting girls and caused by pathogenic variants in an X-linked gene. title: Probing chemical dynamics and catalytic mechanisms in metalloenzymes with advanced X-ray spectroscopy techniques
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIF-LAWRENC BERKELEY LAB
phr: Project Narrative Metalloenzymes are involved in catalyzing many reactions that are of critical importance to health, using transition metals such as Fe or Mn in their active sites. The introduction of X-ray free-electron lasers has made it possible to follow these enzymatic reactions at room temperature in real time as the reaction proceeds. Development of X-ray absorption spectroscopy methods that make use of this novel transformative tool is important for understanding the dynamics of the metal sites and the mechanisms of these reactions.

activity_code: K01
title: Effect of an Anti-Inflammatory Diet on Gut Homeostasis in Active and Experimental Crohn's Disease
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Crohn’s disease (CD) is a chronic, commonly disabling disorder of the gastrointestinal tract that is associated with imbalances in the composition of gut bacteria (‘dysbiosis’), and affects more than 600,000 individuals in the US. Gut bacteria modulation via diet is a needed strategy for the treatment and prevention of CD; however, no specific recommendations exist for CD patients, and little is known about the complex interaction between dietary nutrients, gut bacteria and host immune responses. Understanding these interactions, particularly how specific dietary factors can alleviate disease symptoms through their effect on the composition of gut bacteria, will allow us to develop novel therapeutic approaches to treat, prevent, and ultimately cure the disease.

org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disease that affects 15-30% of children worldwide and predisposes to significant allergic comorbidities, including asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy. A hallmark feature of AD is skin dysbiosis and increased prevalence of Staphylococcus aureus (SA); it is thus crucial to identify mechanisms of persistent microbial colonization in AD that may be targeted clinically for AD prevention and treatment. This proposal focuses on elucidating microbial patterns on AD skin and delineating the mechanisms of SA adhesion, invasion, and persistence over time in early life.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A subset of individuals exhibits resilience to Alzheimer’s disease, as indicated by significant Alzheimer’s disease neuropathology on autopsy despite intact cognition. activity_code: K23
title: Dual-Language Communication and Social-Cognitive Skills in Bilingual Children with ASD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS AMHERST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Bilingual children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD), especially those with minimal spoken language skills, have long been excluded from research and may have limited access to interventions that support their dual- language needs. This project uses a sequential mixed-methods design to examine the dual-language communication and social-cognitive skills of children with ASD with a range of verbal abilities, contextualized by the experiences of their families in making language choices and seeking support services. The long-term goal of this work is to collaborate with families and providers to develop community-based interventions that promote the linguistic and social-cognitive development of bilingual children with ASD.

application_id: 11002343
activity_code: F31
title: Reversal of Age-Associated Damage in the Planarian Germline
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Age-related conditions are among the most devastating and agonizing diseases worldwide, with age- related declines in fertility being among the earliest and most frequent of these conditions. My proposed research studies the mechanisms by which aging damages the germline of the planarian S. mediterranea, and determines how regeneration reverses this damage and restores the flatworms to youthful levels of fecundity. This research will help us understand why humans and other organisms are unable to reverse germline aging, and could lay the groundwork for new treatments that preserve and restore fertility even into advanced age.

title: Mitochondrial mRNA structure as a driver of Leigh Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Leigh’s syndrome is a devastating newborn neurodevelopmental mitochondrial disorder. activity_code: F31
title: Leveraging CRISPR RNA-guided DNA Transposases for Gene Insertion at the CFTR Locus
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite the many exciting advances in gene editing technologies since the advent of CRISPR-Cas9, the field has been hampered by an inability to catalyze programmable and predictable insertion of large DNA payloads without generating double-strand breaks. The recent discovery and development of CRISPR-associated transposons offers an exciting new strategy to insert large genetic cargos (>10kb) with high integration efficiencies and virtually no off-target events. This proposal aims to systematically optimize CRISPR- associated transposons in human cells to achieve therapeutically relevant editing efficiencies, and to apply them for the universal correction of CFTR gene mutations that cause cystic fibrosis.

title: DNA repair pathway coordination during damage processing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Various environmental sources, including exposure to UV light and pollution, relentlessly damage DNA, causing numerous human maladies including neurogenerative diseases and cancer. In this fellowship, I will characterize how three key DNA damage sensors work together to find and handoff oxidative and UV damage in the context of naked DNA and chromatin. activity_code: F31
title: Defining the developmental mechanisms of pericardium formation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The pericardium is the mesothelial sac that surrounds the heart and is essential to support its development, maintain cardiac homeostasis over time, and respond to cardiac injury and infection. However, the developmental origins of pericardial development, and the cellular and molecular mechanisms involved in determining cell fate decisions between the pericardium and other parts of the cardiovascular system remain unknown. This will be the focus of my investigations, and the results from this work will expand our understanding of cardiogenesis and provide a framework to study the homeostatic functions of the pericardium.

org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: Project Narrative Endometriosis increases the risk of cardiovascular disease, and before this, impairs endothelial function. Women with endometriosis demonstrate increased expression of thromboxane A2 a substance which directly induces vasoconstriction, is an antagonist of nitric oxide synthase activity, and sensitizes sensory afferents in pre-clinical models of cardiovascular disease. The proposed research will determine the role of thromboxane A2 in the regulation of vascular tone in women with endometriosis, providing information that can be directly translated to the development of preventive interventions in this population.

application_id: 11009236
activity_code: F31
title: Influences of Stigma and Social Support on Cognitive Function Among Older Women Living with Human Immunodeficiency Virus
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Older women represent an increasing proportion of persons living with HIV in the United States. This research will identify how experiences of stigma impact cognitive function and the extent to which social support can serve as a protective factor against the adverse impacts of stigma. Findings from this study will inform best practices for HIV care and management by potentially highlighting modifiable risk factors (HIV and gender based stigma) that could be targeted to offset cognitive decline among this population.

title: The Impact of an Intervention to Reduce Pharmacists' Racial Bias Towards People of Color at Risk of HIV
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative: People of color are disproportionately affected by HIV and experience substantial disparities in the preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) care continuum. The accessibility of community pharmacies and patient trust in pharmacists suggest that pharmacists can contribute significantly to increasing equity in PrEP care; however, implicit racial bias is common among healthcare providers, including pharmacists, and may reduce the effectiveness of pharmacist-led PrEP delivery. The primary aim of this project is to identify the feasibility, acceptability, and preliminary impact of an adapted evidence-based intervention in reducing implicit racial bias among pharmacists who have experience with or willing to prescribe PrEP in the future.

activity_code: K01
title: Supplement for Neural mechanisms of early visual dysfunction in psychosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative People with psychosis see the world differently than others (e.g., hallucinations), and they do not see some visual illusions as strongly as healthy people do. Because we have a good understanding of visual brain systems from animal research, we can pinpoint the problem with seeing an illusion to a particular neural circuit. This project will use techniques from visual neuroscience along with EEG, magnetic resonance spectroscopy, and behavioral measures to uncover how psychosis disrupts visual perception.

application_id: 11009776
activity_code: K01
title: Antioxidant regulation of intestinal homeostasis and disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative Glutathione peroxidase 1 (GPx1) is one of the most widely expressed antioxidant proteins in the body, yet its role in the intestine is largely unknown. Interestingly, while antioxidant proteins are mostly thought to protect the intestine in the setting of inflammatory bowel disease and colitis-associated cancer, my preliminary data shows that mice without GPx1 are protected from colitis. This proposal will define the role of GPx1 in intestinal epithelial and immune cells, test GPx1-dependent effects on colitis and colitis-associated carcinoma, and evaluate whether GPx1 can serve as a therapeutic target in the setting of gastrointestinal disease.

activity_code: K23
title: Differential Susceptibility for Developing Chronic Post-Surgical Pain Across Sleep Trajectories and Inflammatory Presentations
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Insufficient and poor sleep is pervasive among individuals with shoulder conditions requiring surgical intervention. Shortened sleep and increased sleep disturbances induce an outsized inflammatory reaction, which sensitizes the nociceptive response and may increase susceptibility for developing chronic post-surgical pain and the subsequent need for prolonged postoperative opioid therapy. activity_code: K08
title: Optimizing outcomes for children and young adults with relapse of B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia after CD19-targeted chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy targeting CD19 (CAR19) has revolutionized the treatment landscape for children and young adults with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Unfortunately, however, approximately 50% of children relapse after initial response to CAR19 and their subsequent survival is dismal. This proposal aims to optimize outcomes after post-CAR19 relapse through two mutually informative approaches: 1) A phase 1/2 clinical trial testing the delivery of a novel, dual-antigen targeted CAR designed to overcome the primary mechanisms of CAR19 failure; and 2) An advanced comparative effectiveness study to determine optimal existing salvage treatment approaches for post-CAR19 relapse.

application_id: 11012781
activity_code: K23
title: Relation between acute changes in kidney function with clinical outcomes among patients with heart failure
org_name: TUFTS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE Reduced kidney function is highly prevalent among patients with heart failure, and has been consistently shown to be one of the most powerful risk factors for poor clinical outcomes. Acute declines in kidney function, on the other hand, remain poorly understood and how they are related to outcomes including longer term kidney function decline and cardiovascular events is controversial. This study will examine acute kidney function declines in the context of three critical factors—congestion, cardiac hemodynamics and neurohormonal activity—in order to better understand mechanisms and develop clinical phenotypes of the acute cardiorenal syndrome.

activity_code: F30
title: The Molecular Basis of Nasal Pit Morphogenesis and its Role in Upper Lip Formation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Cleft lip with or without cleft palate, is a leading birth defect worldwide, but the fundamental morphogenesis that drives upper lip formation remains largely mysterious. Our preliminary data indicate that improper nasal pit morphogenesis is a driver of cleft lip and that TGFβ signaling through TGFβRI is a key regulator of this process. The experiments outlined in this application will contribute to understanding the dynamics driving nasal pit morphogenesis as it contributes to lip formation and cleft etiology.

application_id: 11013873
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of Asymmetric Cell Division in Developmental Hematopoiesis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal seeks to elucidate the cellular and molecular mechanisms that control the balance between self-renewal of blood progenitors and differentiation of mature blood cell types. Understanding how this balance is established during normal development and maintained during stress is relevant to blood disorders characterized by the production of disproportionate blood cell types such as fatal myeloproliferative disorder, anemia, and immune dysfunction upon aging. This work will help establish a model system that will allow for real-time monitoring of the blood system and ultimately the identification of key factors that regulate cell division and cell fate during hematopoiesis.

application_id: 11014984
activity_code: K23
title: Characterizing the Biologic Evolution of Crohn's Disease Like Pouch Inflammation in Ulcerative Colitis Patients After Restorative Proctocolectomy with Ileal Pouch Anal Anastomosis
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Up to 30% of patients with treatment refractory ulcerative colitis (UC) require colon removal and the creation of an ileal pouch anal anastomosis (IPAA) to serve as an internal pelvic stool reservoir and allow for defecation per anus. Approximately 20% of patients with an IPAA develop Crohn’s disease-like pouch inflammation (CDLPI) which causes symptoms similar to their original UC, and up to 50% of these patients do not respond to therapy and require surgical pouch removal and the creation of a permanent ostomy. We aim to characterize the genetic variants, ileal transcriptome, microbial and metabolomic profiles associated with CDLPI in patients with UC after IPAA in order to identify predictive disease markers and potential therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11016945
activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of thymic stromal-immune crosstalk for homeostasis and naïve T cell licensing across life
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reduced T cell mediated immunity is a prominent feature of aging with important clinical consequences and is partially driven by diminished thymic function and altered naïve T cell development. Recent evidence suggests heterogeneity amongst naïve T cells that are generated during different developmental windows, but little is known about the intrathymic crosstalk that imprints naïve T cell character. This proposal focuses on understanding the novel role of epithelial-derived IL-13 in establishing medullary thymic function during early adulthood and how these pathways change during thymic aging, which may inform efforts to protect existing thymic function or reverse thymic involution to maximize T cell mediated immunity in the elderly.

activity_code: K01
title: Probing network-specific cortical connectivity by combining multifocal transcranial magnetic stimulation with electroencephalography
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) is a noninvasive brain stimulation method with current FDA approvals for treatment of major depressive disorder (MDD) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) but most clinical applications are focused on single brain target. This project employee a novel multichannel TMS (mTMS) system specifically designed for multifocal brain network stimulation that can be used to enhance the connectivity between brain areas. When combining multifocal brain stimulation with electrical measurements of functional connectivity using electroencephalography (EEG), this technology can be used to optimize the network-level stimulation protocols paving way for improved therapeutic outcomes with minimized side effects and treatment duration in future.

phr: Project Narrative  Cannabis legalization and expansion of availability in commercial retail settings has resulted in increased use among the general population, but its effect on vulnerable groups such as individuals with schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders is not known. org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The consequences of overnutrition on human health are perilous, even though the prevalence of metabolic disease in adults is so high that it may constitute a new normal. title: Johns Hopkins Summer UndergraduateProgram in Kidney Science
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Kidney disease represents a significant growing threat to public health. Unfortunately, efforts to find effective prevention strategies and innovative new treatments have been hampered by a paucity of kidney disease researchers. The Johns Hopkins Summer Undergraduate Program in Kidney Science (SUPerKS) is designed to rebuild recruitment of nephrologists and basic kidney scientists with talented people focusing on undifferentiated undergraduate students through seeding and reinforcing their interest in kidney research.

application_id: 11039921
activity_code: F31
title: Maternal-Child RSA Synchrony in Infants with Fragile X Syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA AT COLUMBIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Maternal-child synchrony describes the ways in which mothers and children impact each other’s social and physiological states, it lays critical groundwork for children’s social development, and it is impacted by contextual factors. Children with fragile X syndrome (FXS) are particularly vulnerable to having disrupted maternal-child respiratory sinus arrythmia (RSA; a marker of cardiac functioning) synchrony due to 1) children with FXS and their mothers experiencing atypical RSA functioning, 2) mothers of children with FXS experiencing elevated mental health challenges, and 3) the clinical status of children with FXS, yet the role of maternal-child RSA synchrony in FXS has not yet been examined and the impact of contextual factors on this RSA synchrony has been neglected. ...

application_id: 11042178
activity_code: F30
title: Determining Sox10-mediated plasticity in irradiated salivary gland cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Over 500,000 head and neck cancer patients receive therapeutic radiation each year, resulting in irreversible damage to the salivary glands, salivary hypofunction, and subsequent xerostomia (dry mouth syndrome). As current strategies to mitigate xerostomia fail to address the underlying mechanism of dysfunction, new therapies that long-term repair the injured gland are necessary to address this unmet clinical need. Given that Sox10 is involved in SG epithelial stem/progenitor cell self-renewal and differentiation processes, here we aim to determine whether Sox10 can induce plasticity in irradiated salivary gland ductal cells.

application_id: 11042868
activity_code: K01
title: Effects of a higher protein diet and resistance training on fat free mass retention during weight loss in adults over 50: A Pilot Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Aging is associated with a progressive accrual of body fat and loss of skeletal muscle mass that causes a vicious cycle of increasing metabolic disease risk and loss of functional capacity. Intentional weight loss improves metabolic health but loss of skeletal muscle during weight loss may cause the development of sarcopenia. The proposed K01 research will use an pilot Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial to begin investigating the separate and combined effects of a higher protein diet and resistance training on the retention of fat free mass during weight loss in adults ≥50 years.

activity_code: K08
title: Immune Regulation of the Blood-Brain Barrier
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project seeks to understand the mechanisms of immune signaling at the blood-brain barrier during neuroinflammation in both animal models of disease and human tissue. The general approach combines the power of a novel model of viral encephalitis, brain vasculature-specific genetic manipulation, and thorough validation in human cases of viral encephalitis. The long-term goal of this research is to generate novel therapeutic targets at the blood-brain barrier to treat the millions of Americans who suffer from neuroinflammatory conditions including viral infection, autoimmune encephalitis, and multiple sclerosis.

title: Sleep and Circadian Rhythms in Adolescence: Epigenetic Pathways of Cardiometabolic Risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adolescence is a vulnerable time period when insufficient and mistimed sleep may affect cardiometabolic risk factors including adiposity, high blood pressure, and insulin resistance. To understand which aspects of sleep to intervene on, there is an urgent need to disentangle the independent contributions of short sleep duration and delayed sleep timing (proxy for circadian rhythm misalignment) to cardiometabolic risk. Examining the epigenetic pathways that link sleep duration and timing with prospective cardiometabolic risk provides a novel way to distinguish these relationships and to gain mechanistic insights.

application_id: 11052735
activity_code: K99
title: Mindfulness-based Intervention for Pain and Sleep in Adolescents and Young Adults with Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Approximate 40% of adolescents and young adults (AYA) with sickle cell disease (SCD) experience chronic pain and 33% of AYA with SCD report sleep deficiency. Despite the growing evidence of effect of mindfulness-based intervention (MBI) for pain and sleep, AYA with SCD are systematically undertreated for pain. This research will provide an age-appropriate MBI for AYA with SCD to reduce pain and sleep deficiency.

application_id: 11052743
activity_code: K01
title: Analysis of C9ORF72-associated poly (GR) interactions on BAF chromatin remodeling complex structure and function
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) are two fatal neurodegenerative diseases, and a repeat expansion mutation in the chromosome 9 open reading frame (C9ORF72) constitutes the most common form of familial FTD and ALS. Repeat expansions can generate five different dipeptide repeat proteins (DPRs), and we have found that these the DPR, poly (GR) can interact with the chromatin remodeling complex BRG1/BRM-associated factor (BAF), inducing a decrease the levels of this chromatin regulator. In this proposal, we will analyze how this interaction affect complex structure and the capacity to modify chromatin status and gene expression in patient-derived iPSCs derived neurons and its effects on neuronal survival and function.

title: Enhancing buprenorphine low dose initiation (COPILOT)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: This research addresses the urgent public health crisis of opioid overdose deaths by developing new strategies to make starting buprenorphine treatment easier and more successful for people using fentanyl. By testing an Enhanced Buprenorphine Initiation program that combines innovative initiation methods with patient-centered supports, this project seeks to improve treatment access, retention, and outcomes. Findings will help expand effective, real-world approaches to reduce overdose deaths and improve the health of individuals with opioid use disorder.

title: Developing Evidence-Based Guidance for Engaging Rural Residents in the Deep South in Genomics Research: A Stakeholder-Driven Perspective
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE

application_id: 11060283
activity_code: K01
title: Developing a Digital Intervention for Adolescent Nonsuicidal Self-injury
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims to develop and evaluate a digital mental health intervention (DMHI) for adolescents who engage in nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) and who are not currently engaged in treatment. I will employ user- centered design methodologies to understand adolescents’ specific needs and preferences, which will guide the development of a scalable, low-intensity DMHI designed to be implemented within the digital offerings of a large mental health advocacy website. The DMHI will support the acquisition and practice of skills to reduce NSSI frequency, and encourage use of external supports to increase further help-seeking among high-risk adolescents who are currently unable or uninterested in accessing traditional psychological treatments.

application_id: 11062145
activity_code: K01
title: Dysfunctional behavior with friends during middle childhood as a precursor to borderline personality pathology - Supplement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a serious mental illness associated with comorbidity across internalizing and externalizing pathology as well as high personal and societal cost. This study will use a high-risk design to assess problematic interpersonal behavior exhibited by children with their friends to determine whether it reflects a precursor to BPD or other internalizing and externalizing problems. Results will provide critical information about hypothesized disease trajectory for BPD, which will inform targets for prevention and early intervention.

application_id: 11063998
activity_code: K01
title: Does Senescence Impair the Cardiovascular Benefits of Menopause Hormone Therapy?
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Narrative: ASCVD progresses with aging and causes one-third of deaths worldwide. The protection in females against ASCVD is lost in postmenopausal women and has not been replicated in clinical trials, in part because the mechanisms are not completely understood. By using animal models of surgical menopause and hormone therapy overlayed with bone marrow transplant techniques, this project will define a novel mechanism to provide a platform for developing therapeutic methods to replicate the protection of sex hormones against ASCVD.

application_id: 11065834
activity_code: F30
title: The impact of host RANKL signaling on Salmonella Typhimurium pathogenesis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Monocytes are unique amongst immune cells in that they can differentiate into bone-resorbing osteoclasts following exposure to the canonical osteoclastogenic cytokine Receptor Activator of Nuclear factor KappaB- Ligand (RANKL). However, it has recently become appreciated that RANKL regulates diverse facets of mammalian physiology outside of the skeleton, including processes like lymphoid organ development and immune cell crosstalk that are critical for host defenses against bacterial pathogens. The objective of this proposal is to define how RANKL-mediated cellular reprogramming alters the antibacterial and inflammatory properties of monocytes to shape the outcome of infectious disease.
terms: Ablation;Agonist;Anti-Bacterial Agents;Antigen-Antibody Complex;Antigens;Bacteria;Bacterial Model;Biogenesis;Biomedical Research;Blocking Antibodies;bone;Bone Resorption;Caspase;Cell Lineage;Cell Reprogramming;Cells;Cellular biology;Clinical;Communicable Diseases;Cues;cytokine;Cytokine Receptors;Data;design;Development;Disease;Disease Outcome;Distant;Environment;Epigenetic Process;epigenetic regulation;experience;Exposure to;FDA approved;Fellowship;Gastroenteritis;Gene Expression Profile;Gene Silencing;Genes;genetic approach;Genetic Transcription;genomic locus;Goals;Hematogenous;Host Defense;Human;Immune;Immune response;Immunity;Immunology;improved;in vivo;Infection;Inflammasome;Inflammatory;Inflammatory Response;Innate Immune Response;Institution;Interleukin-1 beta;Interleukins;intestinal barrier;intestinal homeostasis;Intestines;Invaded;Iron;Knowledge;Laboratories;Leukocytes;Ligands;Literature;lymphoid organ;Macrophage;Measures;Mediating;Medical;Metabolic;Modeling;monocyte;mouse model;Mus;Myelogenous;Natural Immunity;Nuclear;Nutritional;Organ;organ growth;Osteoclasts;osteoimmunology;Outcome;pathogen;Pathogenesis;pathogenic bacteria;Pathway interactions;Phagocytes;Phagolysosome;Phagosomes;pharmacologic;Phenotype;Physiology;polarized cell;Predisposition;Process;Production;programs;Property;protein transport;Proteins;Reaction;receptor;recruit;Regulation;Research;response;Salmonella;Salmonella enterica;Salmonella infections;Salmonella typhimurium;Sampling;Scientist;secondary lymphoid organ;Shapes;Signal Transduction;Skeleton;Stimulus;synergism;Systemic infection;Testing;Tissues;Training;Training Programs;Transcriptional Regulation;transcriptome sequencing;Universities;Vacuole;Virulence

title: ICOS is an important mediator of both resident memory cell formation, and improved durability and persistence of anti-tumor T cells
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Adoptive T cell transfer (ACT) therapy uses the patient’s expanded immune cells to fight cancer and is a promising approach for cancer treatment. Melanoma, a malignancy with low survival rates at later stages, has the potential to be impacted by ACT, and a more robust understanding of how we can improve this therapy is needed. application_id: 11068972
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the chromatin landscape of ALT telomeres
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer cells must activate a telomere maintenance mechanism to continuously proliferate; while 85% of cancer cells reactivate telomerase, 15% utilize a mechanism of homologous recombination termed Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres (ALT). The mechanisms by which ALT is induced and maintained in cancer cells remain unknown. The goal of this proposal is to examine the chromatin dynamics at ALT-positive telomeres, investigate how chromatin state impacts ALT, and assess the potential of leveraging this knowledge for ALT- positive tumor treatments.

application_id: 11070718
activity_code: F32
title: Characterization of metal cofactors involved in GDGT-MAS Csp3-Csp3 bond formation
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: Project Narrative The enzyme-mediated Csp3-Csp3 bond formation between two completely inert carbons was unprecedented until the crystallization and subsequent characterization of Radical S-Adenosyl methionine (RS) enzyme GDGT–macrocyclic archaeol synthase (MAS). The ability to selectively form a Csp3-Csp3 bond between inert carbons is highly sought after in organic chemistry; however, little is known about GDGT-MAS’s associated metal cofactors (three [Fe4S4] clusters and rubredoxin-like Fe) in facilitating this novel biological reaction. This project aims to investigate the role of the rubredoxin Fe and N-terminal domains [Fe4S4] in GDGT-MAS.

application_id: 11071516
activity_code: F31
title: The Interplay Between mTOR Inhibitors and Endothelial Cell-to-Cell Junction Dynamics: Implications for Vascular Barrier Dysfunction
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite their widespread use in transplant medicine and coronary stent intervention to regulate cell growth and proliferation, immunosuppresants, like everolimus cause vascular complications impacting patient care. Recent advancements in these types of drugs, such as torin-2, offer potential solutions, though the mechanisms remain unclear, stalling further innovation in this area. This research proposal will probe how these closely related medications produce different effects on blood vessels by using physiological relevant cell models that measure temporal dynamics to accurately characterized vascular barrier effects in live systems.

application_id: 11071688
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular and Cellular Determinants of Tolerance to Second Generation Antipsychotics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Second generation antipsychotics (SGAs) are widely used clinical tools, but they often take weeks to become effective, and the mechanism of this time course is not understood. Here, using in vivo optical methods, transgenic mice, and receptor radiolabeling, I will determine a mechanism of tolerance development to select SGAs at gross anatomy, cell-type, and protein levels. This will provide critical insight how these drugs alter the dopaminergic circuitry and explain the clinical time course of SGAs and, attribution of SGA tolerance to specific dopaminergic projections could inform the development of better antipsychotic treatments.

title: PREventing Pain after Surgery: A feasibility and acceptability study of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy for the prevention of chronic post surgical pain (PREPS)

activity_code: F99
title: Investigating Visual Attention Mechanisms During Emotional Inferences
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will investigate the neural and behavioral mechanisms of social visual attention using dynamic and context-rich stimuli. The F99 phase will investigate how and where humans make eye movements toward relevant information during dynamic emotion perception and with the use of computer vision models. The K00 phase extends this research by investigating the neural mechanisms of social visual attention using dynamic and context-rich stimuli as well as naturalistic imaging approaches.

application_id: 11077005
activity_code: K01
title: Nicotinic and Insular Cortical Mechanisms Contributing to Opiate Misuse
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative Use of tobacco products is a strong predictor of nonmedical use of prescribed opiates, development of opiate dependence, and susceptibility to accidental opiate overdose. Nicotine use results in insular cortex dysfunction which is further associated with development of substance use disorders. Elucidation of the mechanisms through which nicotine, acting within the insular cortex, may enhance opiate addiction liability is critical for development of treatment strategies aimed at the large proportion of nicotine and opiate polysubstance users currently involved in opiate treatment programs.

title: Dissecting the heterogeneity of oral cancer pain
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Oral cancer is one of the most painful cancers, yet the etiology of oral cancer pain remains unknown. The proposed studies will characterize sensory changes and pain that accompany oral cancer in clinical and preclinical settings. Knowledge gained from the proposed research may facilitate the development of individualized pain therapies to specifically address pain in individual patients.

org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Despite modest strides in decreasing HIV incidence in the US, men continue to face barriers to accessing HIV testing and HIV prevention services compared to women. Dr. Ingrid Bassett has a long and successful track record mentoring trainees and is deeply committed to expanding this mentoring. Working with collaborators in the community in Boston, Dr. Bassett will evaluate strategies for improving access to HIV prevention and other sexual health services for men and mentor the next generation of patient-oriented researchers.

activity_code: F31
title: A Role for TLR2 in Regulating Immune Surveillance in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pancreatic cancer shows remarkable resistance to T cell immunotherapy. This proposal will use mouse models of pancreatic cancer to study the role of toll-like receptor 2 in regulating T cell surveillance in cancer. Findings will improve our understanding of mechanisms that regulate the anti-tumor potential of T cells and may reveal novel therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11088907
activity_code: R25
title: Seeds to STEM: Cultivating early Nutrition, Literacy, Numeracy, and STEM knowledge and skills in Teachers, 3-5 year olds, and Families through a Bilingual Asset-based curriculum in Two Major Cities
org_name: THE ACADEMY OF NATURAL SCIENCES OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Seeds to STEM proposes a program that educates low- income children ages 3-5, their families, and preschool teachers about childhood nutrition, early STEM and literacy, and kindergarten readiness. It has an innovative focus on food access and serves populations in under-accredited early childhood care and learning centers, including home-based childcare providers. Seeds to STEM will be piloted in two major cities (Philadelphia, PA and Los Angeles, CA) and its three product deliverables will be distributed nationwide via a website and multiple professional dissemination networks.

activity_code: K12
title: CTSA K12 Program at Virginia Commonwealth University
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: This K12 training program at the Wright Regional Center for Clinical and Translational Research will train scholars to translate research observations to clinical application in our communities. Our program will continue its commitment to providing early career faculty/late-stage postdoctoral fellows with a comprehensive “blueprint” for transitioning to research independence. We will further leverage our bidirectional partnerships with local institutions (EVMS, ODU, and VSU) to further synergize with internal programs (UM1, T32 and RC2) to promote the development of a research workforce (K12, T32, R25), and to further build a community of scholarship while fostering the development of a pipeline beyond our K12 scholars.

application_id: 11095012
activity_code: K23
title: Reducing Disparities in Pediatric Diabetes: Building the Evidence Base to Inform Effective Diabetes Technology Interventions in Underrepresented Minorities
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Youth from low socioeconomic groups are at a systematic disadvantage in the provision of diabetes care, particularly diabetes technology which is associated with improvement in diabetes-specific outcomes. Thus, we urgently need studies to understand and ameliorate the persistent worsening of disparities of diabetes management and outcomes in youth from low socioeconomic backgrounds. This proposed research will (1) improve representation of diverse youth in the literature, (2) address the gap in knowledge of barriers and promoters in publicly insured youth, and (3) identify and address factors of worsening disparity in diabetes technology.

application_id: 11096519
activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Neuroengineering and Medicine
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Disorders of the nervous system represent one of the largest, fastest growing areas of medical need in the United States and the most promising therapies for these disorders are new technologies to image, analyze, and modulate activity in neural circuits. There is a great need for engineers educated in clinical neuroscience and physicians with technical backgrounds to train and work together, to effectively bring these critical technologies to patient care. The goal of this multidisciplinary program is to train pre-doctoral engineering students, and post-doctoral MD, PhD and MD-PhD fellows together in Neuroengineering research, to translate their work to clinical care, and ultimately improve the lives of individuals with brain and nervous system disorders.

org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Johns Hopkins BIRCWH program takes advantage of an extraordinary interdisciplinary academic environment to support the career development of promising junior scientists. application_id: 11099977
activity_code: K12
title: Building Interdisciplinary Research Careers in Women's Health
org_name: TULANE UNIVERSITY OF LOUISIANA
phr: There is a critical need to determine sex differences in cardiovascular, infectious and neurological diseases and related risk factors across the research spectrum to improve health, eliminate disparities and optimize health care. In order to most effectively tackle the significant problems in women’s health, including sex differences, there needs to be a workforce prepared to undertake interdisciplinary, collaborative research. To this end, the Tulane BIRCWH program will provide mentored career development for junior faculty to increase the number and diversity of highly trained independent investigators engaged in women’s health and sex differences research in the fields of cardiovascular diseases (CVD), infectious diseases (ID), and neurosciences (NS) across the lifespan.

In this supplementary application, we propose a three-year training program for Victoria Yunez Behm, a mater’s trained leader in integrative nutrition who has been accepted into UNC’s nutrition PhD program and is committed to extending multicomponent integrative health interventions to vulnerable communities. application_id: 11105126
activity_code: K23
title: Targeting pathologic macrophage activation through inhibition of MyD88 to attenuate laryngotracheal stenosis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The research proposed in this application will investigate and target the aberrant TLR4-MyD88 signaling pathways that regulate disease promoting macrophages (immune cells) associated with laryngotracheal stenosis. Secondarily, we will investigate the utility of the S100A8/A9 protein as a predictor for the development of laryngotracheal stenosis in patients who require prolonged endotracheal intubation for critical illness.

application_id: 11109399
activity_code: K12
title: Diabetes-Docs: Physician-Scientist Career Development Program (DiabDocs)
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: The prime objective of our National K12 “Diabetes-Docs: Physician-Scientist Career Development Program” (DiabDocs) is to train and foster the development of physician-scientists for productive and impactful diabetes research careers. Our K12 program is designed to provide: 1) a mentored research experience, 2) research project support, 3) career development training, and 4) mentor development and support. To date the program has attracted very high caliber applicants and mentors from 23 unique institutions and is training scholars to have impactful careers in academic medicine as skilled physician-scientist leaders focused on diabetes.

application_id: 11110385
activity_code: K01
title: Evaluating the Implementation of High Intensity Home-Based Rehabilitation Following Stroke
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative A stroke causes significant disruption in the ability of individuals to participate in functional activities that are associated with every-day living. Clinical guidelines recommend rehabilitation to address functional deficits, but the cost of that rehabilitation in skilled nursing and inpatient rehabilitation facilities is becoming increasingly unsustainable. The goal of this project is to examine whether a high intensity home-based rehabilitation approach can be successfully implemented and contribute to functional outcomes similar to those that are achieved in those costly facilities.
terms: N/A
---
application_id: 11110864
activity_code: K99
title: Identifying the mechanism of olfactory receptor gene regulation in olfactory neurons with live-cell imaging
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative The ability to smell is necessary for long-term physical and behavioral health in humans. Epidemiological studies suggest that older adults with impaired olfactory perception have a 4-fold increase in mortality within 5 years, and an increasing number of studies detect significant correlation between olfactory deficits and the future onset of neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer’s. The proposed research is motivated not only by the desire to solve a fascinating molecular mystery that constitutes the keystone of olfaction, but also by the need to obtain insight to a sensory modality critical for human health.

application_id: 11111352
activity_code: K08
title: Implementing breast cancer screening using clinical breast exam in the primary health centers of Abuja, Nigeria
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Women living in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) suffer extremely high mortality if they are diagnosed with breast cancer, in part because women in these regions are more likely to be diagnosed with late-stage cancer. Programs using clinical breast examination (CBE) to screen for breast cancer have been shown to decrease late-stage diagnoses and improve cancer mortality but are difficult to implement in the resource-constrained healthcare systems of LMICs. This research aims to work with primary care providers and patients to develop strategies for implementing CBE screening programs in Nigeria, which is a necessary first step to reducing mortality for Nigerian women diagnosed with breast cancer.

title: Cognitive and Affective Network Dysfunction and Neuromodulation in Aging and Synucleinopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE: Synucleinopathies such as LBD and PDD are some of the most common and fastest growing age-related neurodegenerative diseases in the world, and contribute to mixed pathologies seen in some Alzheimer’s disease. Despite effective treatments for the motor symptoms of tremor, slowness, and stiffness, there are few effective therapies or biomarkers for the cognitive impairment, depression, apathy, and anxiety increasingly recognized as core non-motor features of these diseases. Age is the central risk factor but is rarely studied as a mechanistic contributor in animal models of these cognitive and motivational symptoms. Determining the interaction between aging and synuclein pathology in cognitive and motivational network pathology and symptoms, and testing a non-invasiv...

application_id: 11113139
activity_code: K12
title: Diabetes-Docs: Physician-Scientist Career Development Program (DiabDocs)
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Contact PD/PI: Maahs, David Matthew PROJECT NARRATIVE The prime objective of our National K12 “Diabetes-Docs: Physician-Scientist Career Development Program” (DiabDocs) is to train and foster the development of diverse physician-scientists for productive and impactful diabetes research careers with a specific focus on type 1 diabetes (T1D). Our K12 program is designed to provide: 1) a mentored research experience, 2) research project support, and 3) career development training, including directed attention to diversity, equity and inclusion. We are confident that we will attract the highest caliber of applicants and mentor them toward impactful careers in academic medicine as skilled physician-scientist leaders. Project Narrative Page 9

application_id: 11115010
activity_code: K23
title: Injectable Extended-Release Buprenorphine (XR-B) in a Correctional Setting: A Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial
org_name: RHODE ISLAND HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD) are an effective treatment in the community and among incarcerated individuals, yet they are offered in very few correctional settings nationwide. The criminal justice (CJ) system offers a unique opportunity to engage individuals in treatment initiation, given the high prevalence of opioid use disorder (OUD) in jails and prisons and the high risk of opioid overdose upon release. The study will compare treatment outcomes between extended-release buprenorphine (XR-B) to treatment-as-usual sublingual buprenorphine (SL-B) in a CJ setting and conduct a mixed-methods process evaluation which will characterize the facilitators of and barriers to the implementation of this novel treatment modality.

application_id: 11115026
activity_code: K01
title: Advancing Fungal Disease Epidemiology through Multilevel Modeling and Data Integration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Invasive fungal diseases are a growing public health concern with risk shaped by factors operating across individual, neighborhood, and healthcare system levels. This project will use large-scale clinical and contextual data to examine how factors at the individual, neighborhood, and policy level contribute to risk and outcomes for three major fungal diseases: aspergillosis, candidiasis, and cryptococcosis. The findings will support the development of targeted prevention and treatment strategies by improving our understanding of how contextual factors shape invasive fungal disease risk.

application_id: 11115557
activity_code: T32
title: Understanding Health and Disease at the Behavioral-Biomedical Interface
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: The interface of behavioral and biomedical sciences is one of the most cutting-edge frontiers of science today. The Behavioral-Biomedical Interface Program at the University of Iowa is training the next generation of behavioral researchers to utilize rigorous biomedical methodologies and conceptual frameworks to enable them to make transformative breakthroughs in understanding and addressing mechanisms that influence human health and disease. Training at the Behavioral-Biomedical interface will enable these researchers to innovatively address human health and disease, allowing for improvements in quality of life and public health across the lifespan.

activity_code: K01
title: Implementation of Trauma Informed care for Youth Living with HIV in Memphis, TN.
org_name: MEHARRY MEDICAL COLLEGE
phr: Enhanced interventions are needed to improve HIV appointment adherence and interconnected psychological trauma among youth with HIV in the Southern United States (U.S.). We will adapt and test an HIV-trauma treatment to improve outcomes for both youth with HIV and their HIV care providers in the priority region of Memphis, TN, which houses the fourth highest rate of new HIV infections, with a third among youth. Proposed research activities will lay the foundation for future R-level funding and support a promising young HIV researcher to progress career development goals.

application_id: 11125681
activity_code: K01
title: Improving age-related risk assessment and documentation for diverse older adults with cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Americans over age 65 are the largest group of patients and survivors of cancer in the U.S. and are also the most likely to experience sub-optimal treatment outcomes. Assessment of these factors, as part of a clinical exam, can help reduce risk. However, assessment and documentation of age-related risk is not well- coordinated, putting older adults at greater risk, especially diverse older patients such as those with limited English proficiency. The proposed project seeks to examine medical record and multi-level factors that can improve assessment of age-related risk and documentation with a broader goal of improving treatment planning and care for diverse older adults with cancer.

title: Examining the relationship between caregiving factors predictors of health, and quality of life among parental caregivers of adults with profound autism: A mixed methods study
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: This research study will enhance understanding of how parental caregivers of adults with profound autism rate their quality of life and what factors most significantly impact their quality of life. Adults with profound autism require substantial, 24 hour-a-day, care. This study’s results can be used to identify policies, supports, resources, and interventions which improve parental caregivers’ lives, support them in their role, and help them provide quality care for their adult-child.

application_id: 11128557
activity_code: K23
title: Spreading LOV: Developing a Plan to Improve Engagement in the Life Outside Violence Program
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Violent injury is an under addressed public health threat that disproportionately affects children and young adults in the United States (US). Life Outside Violence (LOV), the St. Louis region-wide hospital-based violence intervention program, is an individualized program that provides counseling and social- and case-based services to promote healing and prevent reinjury and death in patients who have presented to a LOV partner hospital with a violent injury. This project is a multi-center, mixed-methods study of barriers and facilitators of LOV enrollment informed by the social-ecological model that uses community-based participatory research methods with an intervention mapping operational structure to design and assess an improved pathway to engage violently injured patients in the prog...

application_id: 11129405
activity_code: K23
title: Association between early Candida infection (oral thrush) and severe early childhood caries
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Early childhood caries (ECC) remains the most common chronic childhood disease worldwide and causes a major impact on children, families, and healthcare systems, especially in low- and middle-income countries and underserved populations. Candida is frequently detected in the oral cavity of toddlers with severe ECC (S-ECC) and its presence is implicated with an elevated risk for S-ECC, but its role on the etiopathogenesis of the disease remains unclear. This study aims at elucidating whether early Candida infection in children during the first year of life is associated with S-ECC onset and severity in a longitudinal study.
