application_id: 11135924
activity_code: K99
title: Decoding the repair and immunomodulatory functions of lymphatic vessels in wound healing
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysfunction of lymphatic vessels can lead to lymphedema and no-healing ulcers, affecting over 250 million people worldwide. Here I propose to examine the role of repair and immune-modulatory functions of lymphatic vessels in the control of wound repair to further our understanding of the cellular and molecular factors that underlie this complex process. My studies will uncover novel targets aimed at modulating lymphatic vessels, immune cells, and epidermal stem cells to advance therapeutic approaches in pathological tissue repair.

activity_code: K01
title: Stress and aging: longitudinal evaluation of synaptic changes using in vivo SV2A PET
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Converging evidence suggesting that stress, particularly chronic stress, can accelerate brain synaptic aging, which, in turn, may contribute to premature functional and cognitive decline, increased morbidity, and mortality. Here, we propose establishing an in vivo molecular imaging-based approach to perform a longitudinal evaluation of synaptic changes in an animal model of chronic stress and aging to test the hypothesis that stress accelerates brain synaptic aging. Critically, we will use the same in vivo molecular imaging method used in clinical studies to track synaptic changes in our animal model, making findings more immediately translatable, and hopefully facilitate future mechanistic translational research that will improve our understanding and treatment of stres...

title: Cancer Prevention Education: Student Research Experiences
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: This competitive renewal application for years 31 to 35 of an R25 award at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Cancer Prevention Education: Student Research Experiences, requests support for 25 students in 10-week educational experiences each year for 5 years in multidisciplinary mentored cancer prevention research and career development. The Program recruits graduate and undergraduate students from the basic biomedical sciences, biostatistics, epidemiology, genetics, behavioral and social sciences, nursing, medicine, pharmacy, and related public health disciplines. The overall educational objective is to provide short-term research experiences in cancer prevention research to students as a means of preparing them for careers in cancer prevention research.

application_id: 11143220
activity_code: T32
title: Research Training to Promote Scientist Diversity & Health Equity in Cardiovascular & Lung Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: The proposed training program at the University of Texas at Austin is an inter-professional education partnership between the Schools of Social Work and Medicine. The proposed program aims to recruit and retain promising pre- and postdoctoral scholars to train and engage them in behavioral, population, and implementation science research to promote cardiovascular and respiratory health. Key innovations include recruitment and mentoring activities, including a team-based mentoring approach with a near-peer mentoring component, and cross-fertilization of social work and medical trainees.

title: Optimizing implementation of long-acting, injectable Cabotegravir for HIV prevention among adolescents and young adults in Zambia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Long-acting, injectable Cabotegravir human immunodeficiency virus pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) has the potential to prevent infection among those for whom daily, oral PrEP is ineffective, including many adolescents and young adults in Southern and Eastern Africa who continue to experience high incidence. Poorer than expected outcomes from the initial roll-out of oral PrEP globally and limited utilization of promising injectable formulations in other disease areas demonstrate that implementation success is far from certain, however. This study will identify long-acting, injectable Cabotegravir PrEP health system implementation opportunities, measure health worker and youth implementation preferences, identify preference heterogeneity, and specify implementation strategies to optimize pre...

title: IRACDA at the University of Pennsylvania: Penn Postdoctoral Opportunities in Research and Teaching (PennPORT).
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: To maintain continued healthcare innovation, it is essential to educate students from a broad range of backgrounds, providing all with knowledge and an appreciation for scientific thinking, and inspiring some to pursue science careers. phr: This project aims to develop engagement strategies translated from behavioral economics (i.e., episodic future thinking, reciprocity) to increase engagement of emerging adults (EAs; ages 18 to 29 years) who engage in binge and heaving alcohol use and are susceptible to academic difficulties in a mindfulness mobile health (mHealth) app. We will investigate the feasibility and acceptability of translating and integrating strategies drawn from behavioral economics in an mHealth intervention to support engagement in mHealth mindfulness (proximal outcome) and reduction in alcohol use (distal outcome). application_id: 11160063
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of Neurodegeneration in KIF5A ALS/FTD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Gain of function mutations in motor protein KIF5A are an emerging cause of pathogenesis in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) and Frontotemporal Degeneration (FTD). This proposal investigates how ALS/FTD causative mutations in KIF5A affect its function in axonal transport and cytoskeletal regulation. Our findings will provide insights into the critical roles KIF5A in neuronal health and lay the foundation for rational design of therapeutics to treat KIF5A ALS/FTD.

application_id: 11160188
activity_code: D43
title: Southern African-Pittsburgh Public Health Genomic Epidemiology Training Program (SAPPHGenE)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Public Health Relevance: We propose to continue the highly-successful Southern Africa-Pittsburgh Public Health Genomic Epidemiology Research Training Program (SAPPHGenE) in the areas of bacterial pneumonia, invasive bacterial diseases, respiratory viruses, and fungal infections. This will be accomplished by an expert, multidisciplinary team of research mentors at the University of Pittsburgh and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in Atlanta. The ultimate goal of the program is to prevent and control these diseases, some of which are vaccine preventable and all which are major causes of morbidity and mortality in the United States, Southern Africa, and globally.

title: Miami Clinical and Translational Science Institute K12 Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: This program will prepare the next generation of scientists, medical doctors, and future leaders to be able to solve the most pressing health problems in South Florida and beyond. In the two-year program 10 young scientists will receive in-depth training and guidance from experienced scientist’s, physicians, and other stakeholders who will help them become outstanding independent researchers. This program is designed to meet national needs to increase the numbers of outstanding scientists who can better serve the health needs of our communities

application_id: 11162104
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting regenerative reprogramming in colorectal cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Accumulating evidence indicate that a small subset of tumor cells readily adapt to different types of cellular stresses, including anticancer drugs, ultimately giving rise to tumors for which no effective treatments exist. Using different innovative technologies, this project aims to understand the mechanisms through which cancer cells escape treatment, and to develop approaches that reverse these adaptations, restrain the growth of resistant cells, and improve the outcomes of patients with colorectal cancer and other malignancies.

application_id: 11163525
activity_code: K23
title: Determining the Influence of Clinicodemographic, Biologic and SDOH Factors in Racial and Ethnic Disparities in the Prognosis of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Alcohol-associated liver disease (ALD) is the most common cause of death from liver disease in the United States, accounting for over 20,000 deaths annually. We will investigate how clinical factors (such as alcohol use patterns and metabolic comorbidities) and biological factors (such as genetic variation) influence the course and outcomes of ALD. This work will generate critical data on the natural history and predictors of ALD progression, providing a foundation for improved risk stratification, patient care, and clinical trial design

title: Assessing and Promoting Sexual Health for Patients at Risk for Sexually Transmitted Infections: A Mixed Methods Study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Public Health Relevance: Many patients experience changes affecting their vaginal health such as thinning of the vaginal epithelium, inflammation, and unfavorable shifts in the vaginal microbiota which can lead to bacterial vaginosis. Such changes, in conjunction with sexual behaviors, are associated with negative sexual health outcomes such as increased risk of HIV and sexually transmitted infection acquisition and symptoms such as irritation, discharge, and dyspareunia. application_id: 11165197
activity_code: F30
title: Disentangling the roles of endoplasmic reticulum stress and activity in the peripheral olfactory circuit
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cells in the brain and nervous system develop using a variety of signaling cues, including stress and activity. The effects of these two mechanisms are intertwined in olfactory neurons, the cells in the nose responsible for smell (a sense that is often affected in patients with neurological diseases). We are proposing to disentangle the roles of these two signals across different stages of the olfactory neuron lifespan.

application_id: 11167744
activity_code: K01
title: Characterizing the sex-specific genetic architecture of alcohol use disorders with comorbid major depressive disorder
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Co-morbid Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) increases risk for Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) and unique genetic factors may contribute to AUD risk depending on MDD status. Furthermore, men and women display disparate patterns of temporal development of AUD with co-morbid MDD and their genetic liabilities for the disorders may be different. This project therefore proposes a plan of research considering innovative models to analyze sex-specific genetic and environmental effects on AUD at the item level to contribute to filling the current gap in scientific understanding of the mechanisms by which MDD and sex effects interact with common variants genetic signal to predispose to AUD.

application_id: 11168901
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying mechanisms underlying sex differences in motoneuron discharge
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative The study proposed in this application exhibits substantial promise to quantify motoneuron physiology in the underrepresented population of females and identify a mechanism behind sex differences in motoneuron discharge. Additionally, this is the first quantitative study to examine motoneuron discharge properties across the menstrual cycle. The proposed paradigm is positioned to increase knowledge on female motoneuron physiology, and aid in the understanding of what causes sex differences in the progression of neurological disease.

application_id: 11171576
activity_code: R25
title: My Home, My Health: Place-Based Public Health Resources for Rural Educators
org_name: MONTANA STATE UNIVERSITY - BOZEMAN
phr: My Home, My Health: Place-based Public Health Resources for Rural Educators will research how to attract middle school students to STEM careers. This project will move past traditional information deficit models of the public understanding of science towards a public engagement with science that fosters a multi-directional dialogue incorporating varied perspectives and responding to societal issues and concerns. INBRE researchers, alongside the communities they work with, will help build locally relevant, place-based educational kits focusing on disease ecology. These kits will improve public health awareness and provide a model for how INBRE networks can train their students to bring their research to youth in authentic and engaging ways.

application_id: 11172453
activity_code: T32
title: Translational Cancer Biology (TCB) T32 Training Program
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: This T32 application describes a comprehensive recruitment, training and retention program that combines a strong basic science curriculum with a strong translational cancer focus that distinguishes it from other training programs. Our goals are to train top-quality scientists capable of conducting independent cancer research; foster the intellectual, technical and communication skills required to succeed in the academic or industrial arenas of today and in the future; and to provide an understanding of the basic, public health, and clinical problems of human cancer. The Cancer Center Director of our NCI Designated Comprehensive Cancer Center provides substantial financial support for education and training.

application_id: 11173584
activity_code: K01
title: Emotion processing and risk for anxiety and depression in autistic adults
org_name: GEORGE MASON UNIVERSITY
phr: The goal of the proposed research is to illuminate cognitive and neural factors associated with the elevated risk for co-occurring anxiety and depression in young adults (age 18-30 years) with autism spectrum disorder [ASD]. Structural and functional brain imaging, measures of emotion processing, and ecological momentary assessment will identity factors related to co-occurring anxiety and depression, and will examine the potential role of biological sex. This study will advance understanding of cognitive and brain mechanisms related to elevated risk for anxiety and depression in ASD and inform interventions that will reduce the burden of these serious mental illnesses for all ASD people.

application_id: 11174112
activity_code: K12
title: UIC Building Interdisciplinary Research Careers in Women's Health Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Aligning with the 2019-23 Trans-NIH Plan for Women’s Health Research, the long-term objective of the UIC BIRCWH is to promote training and careers to develop a well-trained, diverse, and robust workforce to advance science for the health of women. Through evidence-based mentoring practices, innovative training strategies, and strong internal and external multidisciplinary collaborations, the UIC BIRCWH improves recruitment, retention, and advancement of women and URMs as scientists and academic leaders in interdisciplinary women’s health and sex/gender differences research.

title: Malawi HIV Implementation Research Scientist Training Program (MHIRST)
org_name: KAMUZU UNIVERSITY OF HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Since 2015, the Malawi HIV Implementation Research Scientist Training program (M-HIRST), a collaborative program between the University of North Carolina and the Kamuzu University of Health Sciences (KUHeS), has established a pipeline of implementation scientists at the Master’s, PhD, Post-Doctoral and Faculty level at KUHeS, its research partners and the Malawi Ministry of Health (MOH). Building on its success, the program will continue its effective implementation science training model while incorporating advanced implementation science and data science skills building for Malawian trainees. The program, seamlessly transitioned to KUHeS leadership, will be poised to address key emerging topics through sustainable local leadership within the Malawi HIV program.

application_id: 11177777
activity_code: K23
title: Examining Loss of Control Eating in Black Adolescents with Overweight/Obesity: The Role of Social Contexts and Social Stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CONNECTICUT STORRS
phr: Rates of obesity for Black adolescents are among the highest of any racial or ethnic group in the United States, and loss of control over eating (LOC; being unable to control what or how much one is eating) confers greater risk for the development and maintenance of excess weight. The goal of the present study is to use a synthesis of qualitative data and ecological momentary assessment to understand the social contexts in which LOC occurs in Black adolescents, and specifically to probe the association between social stress and LOC. This innovative research project has the potential to inform prevention and treatment programs that address both obesity and LOC and are effective in youth from all cultural backgrounds.

application_id: 11177819
activity_code: K01
title: Mapping social network disruptions, HIV, and intervention strategies among women with multistate living patterns in the U.S.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Interstate relocation significantly affects HIV transmission and other health outcomes and has been increasing among U.S. populations over the past two decades. This research focuses on women with multistate living patterns, who face elevated HIV infection rates and are more likely to discontinue preventive HIV care and treatment after relocating. The intervention developed through this study will leverage social networks to strengthen connections to disease prevention and treatment services, addressing health challenges that vary across geographic regions and populations.

activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Cancer Prevention and Control
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: The T32 Cancer Prevention and Control (CPC) program is one of the longest-standing NCI-funded training programs at Massey Comprehensive Cancer Center at Virginia Commonwealth University. It has a proven history of training successful investigators in cancer prevention and control science. The program’s focus on basic and population cancer health science fills a gap in the field by training a cadre of new investigators to further science in this area.

application_id: 11180358
activity_code: T32
title: Cancer Prevention and Control Research Training and Career Development Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: The sixth renewal of the University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston (UTHealth) School of Public Health training program builds on a 29-year track-record preparing fellows to become independent, skilled, and principled cancer control scientists. The training program is designed to prepare cancer prevention and control researchers to fill evidence gaps about multilevel causes of cancer outcomes; to develop and evaluate cancer control multilevel interventions (MLIs); and develop and test strategies to increase the adoption, implementation, sustainment, and scale up of effective interventions in community and health care settings. Ultimately, the long-term goal of the proposed training program is to reduce cancer morbidity and mortality and improve cancer outcomes.
terms: <Cancer Control Research><Program Development><Research Training><cancer prevention><career development>

application_id: 11180364
activity_code: F99
title: Pneumonia-induced microglial activation causes blood-brain barrier breakdown, astrocyte activation, and tau pathology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH ALABAMA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neurovascular unit dysfunction is seen in many neurological disorders, including post-pneumonia delirium and migraine. In this project, I will determine how bacterial pneumonia infection, specifically, if pneumonia-induced microglia activation first causes neurovascular unit dysfunction in vivo in experimental models, and differences in neurovascular unit state in pneumonia vs. non-pneumonia post-mortem human brain. For my postdoctoral project, I will utilize the methods I master during my PhD training to investigate the mechanisms of neurovascular dysfunction at the capillary level in migraine mouse models.

application_id: 11180500
activity_code: K01
title: A behavioral intervention for African-American gay and bisexual men living with HIV to address stigma-related barriers to antiretroviral therapy adherence
org_name: SUNY DOWNSTATE MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Mounting evidence shows that stigma interferes with HIV medication adherence, yet interventions to mitigate its effects are limited, particularly for populations disproportionately affected by the epidemic, such as African-American gay and bisexual men. The proposed project will design and pilot a technology-enhanced stigma intervention to promote HIV medication adherence and viral suppression and reduce chronic inflammation,  morbidity, mortality, and the risk of transmission to HIV-negative partners. The intervention will be evaluated among African-American gay and bisexual men living with HIV, with the long-term aim of adapting and scaling it to other populations and chronic health conditions.

title: OSU Summer Internship Program in Biomedical Informatics and Data Science
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: A strong workforce requires encouraging mentors, interesting projects and engaging opportunities. This summer experience will capitalize on experts in the field to grow undergraduate interest in biomedical informatics and data science and encourage engagement in professional experiences that solve complex public health problems. application_id: 11182520
activity_code: K08
title: Developing a digital symptom monitoring intervention to improve adherence to endocrine therapy among breast cancer survivors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Almost two-thirds of breast cancers are the hormone-receptor positive subtype which are treated with oral endocrine therapy (ET), yet up to 50% of patients do not take these medications as prescribed resulting with increased rates of breast cancer recurrence and death. This new mHealth, intervention, designed with input from breast cancer survivors, will address a major barrier to ET adherence, symptoms from ET. This intervention has great promise for reducing cancer-related symptoms burden which negatively impact quality of life and potentially improving cancer outcomes among breast cancer survivors.

application_id: 11187046
activity_code: R25
title: The Biomedical Informatics and Behavioral Sciences (BIBS) Summer Research Program
org_name: TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCE CTR
phr: The Biomedical Informatics Behavioral Sciences (BIBS) Summer Research Program targets exemplary undergraduate- and graduate-level students and provides stipends for those interested in oral and public health to complete 9 weeks of coursework and applied research experiences. There are some population groups that suffer disproportionate levels of dental diseases, for reasons that are not always well understood, and it is our premise that a dearth of oral health researchers with proficiency in biomedical informatics contributes to this knowledge gap. The BIBS program will offer short-term training in bioinformatics and data science, mentoring, and research experiences to a new cadre of students interested in advanced degrees in oral health research as well as careers in the field.

title: Protective Factors of Cognitive Aging among Latinos in the 50 States and Puerto Rico
org_name: RUSH UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Latinos living in the 50 United States (US) have about 1.5 times the risk of Alzheimer’s disease as non-Latino whites with Puerto Ricans representing the Latino background with the highest prevalence of mild cognitive impairment and cardiovascular disease risk factors, both long associated with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) risk. The overall research and training goals of this K01 center around investigating protective factors to promote healthy cognitive and brain aging in middle-aged and older Latinos (ages 45+) and expand work currently being conducted in the continental US to the US territory of Puerto Rico. Findings may inform the development of culturally-targeted interventions to support healthy cognitive and brain aging in Latinos, the largest r...

org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Fibrotic ILD is a progressive disease characterized by increased extracellular matrix (ECM) stiffness and decreased NLRP3 inflammasome activities, leading to ineffective host defense to infection. activity_code: R25
title: Transforming Analytical Learning in the Era of Big Data: A Summer Institute in Biostatistics and Data Science
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Big Data and Artificial Intelligence is permeating each discipline, including public health. Building on the success of last ten years, we propose to host a six-week undergraduate summer institute in Biostatistics and Data Science: “Transforming Analytical Learning in the Era of Big Data” to be held for the next two summers (2025-2026) at the Department of Biostatistics, Yale School of Public Health, with a group of approximately 25-30 undergraduate students. The trainees will be taught and mentored by a team of interdisciplinary faculty from Biostatistics, Statistics, Biomedical Data Science, Computer Science and Engineering, Epidemiology, Social Sciences and Medicine, reflecting the shared intellectual landscape needed for biomedical and public health Big Data research. application_id: 11229304
activity_code: K23
title: Neuroimaging Phenotypes of Post-Traumatic Epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative Epilepsy is a chronic, debilitating neurological disorder that affects millions of people across the globe. Epilepsy due to head injury, known as post-traumatic epilepsy, represents an opportunity to understand the changes in the brain that lead to epilepsy. This research will help us understand the unique structural and functional changes in the brain leading to post-traumatic epilepsy and develop tools for epilepsy risk stratification after head injury—a prerequisite for feasible clinical trials of therapies for the prevention of epilepsy.

application_id: 11233640
activity_code: F99
title: p27pTpT drives cancer-promoting inflammation and shapes the tumor microenvironment (TME) toward a more tumor-permissive state in vivo
org_name: GEORGETOWN UNIVERSITY
phr: Therapy resistance is a major challenge to the development of effective cancer treatment, therefore, the long- term goal of both the F99 and K00 phases of this work is to elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying therapy resistance. My thesis work identified that p27 can transcriptionally co-regulate STAT3 to activate a gene programs driving cancer stem cell expansion. In this F99, I will investigate if p27 cooperates with STAT3 to promote tumor immune evasion by re-shaping the TME, and the K00 will elucidate if p27 promotes chronic inflammation in obesity by coactivating ER/NFB/STAT3 to increase immune evasion and endocrine therapy resistance in ER+ BC and further test if endocrine blockade/checkpoint inhibitor (anti-PD1) combinational therapy would be beneficial in obese breast canc...

phr: The Michigan Institute for Clinical and Health Research at the University of Michigan seeks to develop a new postdoctoral T32 training program to prepare early-stage postdoctoral fellows for impactful careers in translational science and research. activity_code: K23
title: Developing and piloting a multilevel intervention to address psychosocial and structural syndemics in people with HIV in South Africa
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In South Africa, the country with the highest HIV prevalence (19%), comorbid problems such as depression, post-traumatic stress, and food insecurity synergistically enhance one another (i.e., syndemic problems) and are associated with worse HIV outcomes such as poorer antiretroviral therapy (ART) adherence and worse viral load. This study proposes to: 1) explore the complex interrelationships between syndemic problems and explore attitudes to potential intervention components; 2) develop a multilevel intervention to address syndemic problems and improve adherence (CBT-SA); 3a) assess the feasibility and acceptability of CBT-SA in a pilot RCT; and 3b) identify barriers and facilitators of CBT-SA engagement. If the CBT-SA intervention is found to be feasible and acceptable,...

activity_code: K01
title: CE25-029 - Pathways Between Child Maltreatment and Self-Directed Violence: A Longitudinal, Population-Based Study Using Machine Learning Approaches
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: This project aims to investigate the relationship between child maltreatment and self-directed violence among children and adolescents. By leveraging linked population-level administrative data from child welfare records, medical records, and vital statistics, the research will explore how different types of maltreatment and child welfare interventions contribute to SDV risk. I will also engage in career development and training activities that will enhance my expertise in using large-scale longitudinal birth cohorts by learning and applying machine learning approaches and cross-cutting violence prevention strategies to investigate interpersonal violence amongst children.

org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: Taking discoveries in the laboratory and using them to improve human health requires researchers specifically trained to work collaboratively with other scientists as well as with patients, caregivers, clinicians, and other healthcare stakeholders. application_id: 11280843
activity_code: K01
title: Novel Mechanisms Regulating Renal Perfusion and Kidney Redox Biology: Role in Salt Sensitive Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE PPARγ is an important transcription factor which has anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in the kidney and provides protection against risk factors such as a high salt diet. Little is known about the downstream pathways that PPARγ activates in renal microvessels and their mechanisms of action in combating oxidative stress and promoting sodium excretion. We hypothesize that PPARγ regulates renal hemodynamics and salt sensitivity through suppressing prostaglandin E receptor 3 expression and controlling redox balance in renal microvessels and when impaired induces renal dysfunction leading to salt sensitive hypertension.
terms: absorption;Academia;Animals;antagonist;Antiinflammatory Effect;Antioxidants;Arteries;arteriole;Attenuated;Biological Availability;Biology;blood glucose regulation;Blood Pressure;blood pressure reduction;Blood Vessels;Cardiovascular system;Cells;Cerebrovascular Circulation;Chronic Kidney Failure;Clinical Research;cofactor;Compensation;Data;Development;Dinoprostone;Dominant-Negative Mutation;early onset;Epithelium;Equilibrium;Excess Dietary Salt;Excretory function;Exhibits;Functional disorder;Furosemide;Generations;Genes;Genetic;Goals;Grant;hemodynamics;high salt diet;Human;Hypertension;Impairment;improved;Individual;innovation;Interlobular Artery;Kidney;kidney dysfunction;kidney vascular structure;Ligands;Macrophage;Mediating;Mentored Research Scientist Development Award;Mentors;Metabolic syndrome;Modeling;mortality;Mus;Mutation;Myocardial Infarction;Natriuresis;Nitric Oxide;Nitric Oxide Synthase;Non-Insulin-Dependent Diabetes Mellitus;NOS1 gene;NOS2A gene;novel;Oxidation-Reduction;Oxidative Stress;pathogen;Pathway interactions;Perfusion;pharmacologic;Pharmacology;Physiology;PPAR gamma;prevent;Production;programs;Prostaglandin E Receptor;Prostaglandins;Protein Isoforms;Publishing;Reactive Oxygen Species;receptor;Receptor Signaling;Renal Blood Flow;Research;Resistance;response;Risk Factors;Role;salt intake;salt sensitive hypertension;Scientist;selective expression;Signal Transduction;skills;Sodium;Sodium Chloride;Stroke;Superoxides;symporter;Testing;Thiazolidinediones;transcription factor;Transcriptional Regulation;Tubular formation;Vascular Endothelium;Vascular resistance;Vascular Smooth Muscle;Vasodilation;Water

activity_code: K08
title: A digital health parent coaching intervention to improve neurodevelopmental outcomes for young childhood cancer survivors in underserved communities
org_name: RESEARCH INST NATIONWIDE CHILDREN'S HOSP
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: As more young children survive cancer, they are at risk for substantial late effects from treatment, which lead to a lifetime of healthcare costs, educational delays, and significant public health burden. Given the lack of accessible and tailored early interventions, this study will engage caregivers and healthcare providers in the co-design of a contextually responsive digital health parent coaching intervention to optimize long-term neurodevelopmental and academic outcomes for young childhood cancer survivors and inform more effective supportive care. This is aligned with the NIH’s mission to reduce the burdens of illness and disability, and NCI’s mission to help all people live longer and healthier lives.

application_id: 11292534
activity_code: K23
title: Improving shared decision-making around the use of disease modifying anti-rheumatic drugs in patients with rheumatoid arthritis and cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: NARRATIVE Patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) have an increased incidence of cancer, especially lymphoma and lung cancer, compared to the general population and to date relatively little is known about the safety of standard immunosuppressives for RA in patients with active or recently (< 5 years since diagnosis) diagnosed cancer. Little is also known about how to support decision-making for these patients who have at least two major comorbid conditions and may be under the care of multiple different specialists. The central focus of this proposal is to advance the evidence-base around use of immunosuppressive medications among patients with RA who have active or recently diagnosed cancer and to provide a foundation for shared decision making to ensure alignment between treatment decis...

title: Cost-Effectiveness of HIV Testing in U.S. Emergency Departments
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Emergency departments (EDs) provide a growing amount of all care in the U.S. but, compared with other settings, have some of the lowest rates of HIV testing; decision makers in EDs cite several barriers to more routine HIV testing, including concerns about cost and the impact of increased testing on overcrowding and boarding. Given the public health benefits of more routine HIV screening, Dr. Bennett proposes to investigate the cost-effectiveness of ED-based HIV screening, to create a typology of HIV screening strategies used by 28 local EDs that recently received funding to implement or expand routine, opt-out HIV screening, and to illuminate barriers and facilitators encountered during the real-world implementation of HIV screening programs by employing semi-structured interviews of fron...
